Chapter 1: Statistical Manifolds and the Fisher Metric

CROSS-004

1 Chapter 1: Statistical Manifolds and the Fisher Metric

The fundamental insight of information geometry is that parametric statistical models can be understood as smooth manifolds, where each point corresponds to a probability distribution. This geometric perspective transforms statistical inference from algebraic manipulation into geometric intuition, revealing deep connections between statistics, differential geometry, and optimization theory. The Fisher information matrix emerges naturally as a Riemannian metric on this manifold, giving statistical models an intrinsic geometric structure that governs both inference and learning.

1.1 1.1 Statistical Models as Manifolds

Consider a parametric family of probability distributions indexed by parameters θ ∈ Θ ⊆ ℝ^n. We assume throughout this chapter that the parameter space Θ is an open subset of Euclidean space, ensuring smooth coordinate charts exist globally.

Definition 1.1 (Statistical Manifold). A statistical manifold is a smooth manifold M equipped with a family of probability measures {P_θ : θ ∈ M} such that the map θ ↦ P_θ is smooth in the sense that all moments and cumulants vary smoothly with respect to the manifold structure.

For parametric families with densities p(x; θ) with respect to a common dominating measure μ, we require that p(x; θ) is smooth in θ for μ-almost all x, with derivatives and integrals interchangeable under appropriate regularity conditions.

Definition 1.2 (Regular Statistical Model). A parametric family {p(x; θ) : θ ∈ Θ} is regular if: 1. The parameter space Θ is open in ℝ^n 2. The support {x : p(x; θ) > 0} is independent of θ 3. The log-likelihood ℓ(θ; x) = log p(x; θ) is twice continuously differentiable in θ 4. Differentiation under the integral sign is justified: ∂/∂θ_i ∫ p(x; θ) dμ(x) = ∫ ∂p(x; θ)/∂θ_i dμ(x)

These regularity conditions ensure that the statistical manifold has a smooth structure compatible with the probability distributions.

Example 1.1 (Gaussian Location Family). Consider the family of normal distributions N(μ, 1) with unit variance and varying mean μ ∈ ℝ. The density is:

p(x; μ) = (2π)^(-1/2) exp(-(x-μ)²/2)

This forms a one-dimensional statistical manifold where each point μ corresponds to a Gaussian distribution. The manifold structure is simply ℝ with its standard smooth structure.

Example 1.2 (Multinomial Simplex). The k-dimensional multinomial distributions with probabilities (p₁, …, p_k) subject to Σᵢ pᵢ = 1 and pᵢ > 0 form the interior of the (k-1)-simplex. This is a (k-1)-dimensional manifold embedded in ℝ^k. We can parameterize this using the first k-1 coordinates θᵢ = pᵢ for i = 1, …, k-1, with p_k = 1 - Σᵢ₌₁^(k-1) θᵢ.

1.2 1.2 The Score Function and Fisher Information

The score function captures the sensitivity of the log-likelihood to parameter changes and forms the foundation of the Fisher information metric.

Definition 1.3 (Score Function). For a regular statistical model with log-likelihood ℓ(θ; x) = log p(x; θ), the score function is the gradient: s(θ; x) = ∇_θ ℓ(θ; x) = ∇_θ log p(x; θ)

The score function has mean zero under the true distribution:

Lemma 1.4. For a regular statistical model, E_θ[s(θ; X)] = 0.

Proof. Using regularity condition (4):

E_θ[s(θ; X)] = ∫ ∇_θ log p(x; θ) p(x; θ) dμ(x)
               = ∫ ∇_θ p(x; θ) dμ(x)
               = ∇_θ ∫ p(x; θ) dμ(x)
               = ∇_θ 1 = 0

□

The covariance matrix of the score function defines the Fisher information matrix.

Definition 1.5 (Fisher Information Matrix). The Fisher information matrix is: I(θ) = E_θ[s(θ; X) s(θ; X)ᵀ] = Cov_θ[s(θ; X)]

Since the score has mean zero, this covariance equals the second moment. Under additional regularity conditions, the Fisher information has an alternative form:

Theorem 1.6. For a regular statistical model where second derivatives can be taken under the integral sign: I(θ) = -E_θ[∇²_θ ℓ(θ; X)]

Proof. The (i,j)-entry of the Hessian is:

-E_θ[∂²ℓ/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ] = -∫ ∂²log p(x;θ)/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ p(x; θ) dμ(x)

Computing the second derivative of log p:

∂²log p/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ = ∂/∂θⱼ[1/p · ∂p/∂θᵢ]
                  = -1/p² · ∂p/∂θᵢ · ∂p/∂θⱼ + 1/p · ∂²p/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ

Therefore:

-E_θ[∂²ℓ/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ] = ∫[1/p² · ∂p/∂θᵢ · ∂p/∂θⱼ - 1/p · ∂²p/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ] p dμ
                     = ∫ 1/p · ∂p/∂θᵢ · ∂p/∂θⱼ dμ - ∫ ∂²p/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ dμ
                     = E_θ[∂ℓ/∂θᵢ · ∂ℓ/∂θⱼ] - ∂²/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ ∫ p dμ
                     = E_θ[∂ℓ/∂θᵢ · ∂ℓ/∂θⱼ] - 0

□

Example 1.3 (Fisher Information of Gaussian Family). For the Gaussian location family p(x; μ) = (2π)^(-1/2) exp(-(x-μ)²/2), we have:

ℓ(μ; x) = -½log(2π) - ½(x-μ)²

The score function is: s(μ; x) = dℓ/dμ = x - μ

Therefore: I(μ) = E_μ[(X - μ)²] = Var_μ[X] = 1

The Fisher information is constant, reflecting the translation invariance of the Gaussian family.

1.3 1.3 The Fisher Metric as a Riemannian Structure

The Fisher information matrix I(θ) defines a Riemannian metric on the statistical manifold when it is positive definite.

Definition 1.7 (Fisher Information Metric). The Fisher information metric (or Fisher-Rao metric) on a statistical manifold is the Riemannian metric tensor g defined at each point θ by: g_θ(v, w) = v^T I(θ) w for tangent vectors v, w ∈ T_θM.

This metric has a coordinate-free interpretation in terms of statistical distinguishability.

Theorem 1.8 (Coordinate-Free Fisher Metric). Let L₁, L₂ be two probability measures on a statistical manifold with densities p₁, p₂ respectively. If p₂ = p₁ + ε∇_θp₁|_θ · v + O(ε²) for a unit tangent vector v, then: 4∫ ((p₂ - p₁)²)/(p₁ + p₂) dμ = ε²g_θ(v,v) + O(ε³)

This shows that the Fisher metric measures the second-order distinguishability between nearby distributions via the Hellinger distance.

Example 1.4 (Fisher Metric on Multinomial Simplex). For the multinomial family with parameters θ = (p₁, …, p_(k-1)) and p_k = 1 - Σᵢ pᵢ, the log-likelihood for observation x = (n₁, …, n_k) is:

ℓ(θ; x) = Σᵢ₌₁^(k-1) nᵢ log pᵢ + n_k log(1 - Σⱼ pⱼ)

The score components are: ∂ℓ/∂pᵢ = nᵢ/pᵢ - n_k/p_k

The Fisher information matrix has entries: I_ij(θ) = E[∂ℓ/∂pᵢ · ∂ℓ/∂pⱼ] = δᵢⱼ/pᵢ + 1/p_k

where δᵢⱼ is the Kronecker delta. This can be written as: I(θ) = diag(1/p₁, …, 1/p_(k-1)) + (1/p_k)1·1^T

where 1 is the vector of ones.

1.4 1.4 The Cramér-Rao Bound as a Geometric Inequality

The Cramér-Rao bound, fundamental to statistical inference, has a natural geometric interpretation as a statement about the length of vectors in the Fisher metric.

Theorem 1.9 (Cramér-Rao Bound). Let T(X) be any unbiased estimator of a function τ(θ), and suppose τ is differentiable. Then: Cov_θ[T(X)] ≥ (∇τ(θ))^T I(θ)^(-1) (∇τ(θ)) where the inequality is in the positive semidefinite sense.

Proof. Since T is unbiased, E_θ[T(X)] = τ(θ). Differentiating both sides:

∇_θ E_θ[T(X)] = ∇τ(θ)

Under regularity conditions:

∇_θ ∫ T(x) p(x; θ) dμ(x) = ∫ T(x) ∇_θ p(x; θ) dμ(x)
                            = ∫ T(x) p(x; θ) ∇_θ log p(x; θ) dμ(x)
                            = E_θ[T(X) s(θ; X)]

Therefore: E_θ[T(X) s(θ; X)] = ∇τ(θ)

By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

|E_θ[T(X) s(θ; X)]|² ≤ E_θ[T(X)T(X)^T] · E_θ[s(θ; X)s(θ; X)^T]

Since E_θ[T(X)] = τ(θ), we have:

Cov_θ[T(X)] = E_θ[T(X)T(X)^T] - τ(θ)τ(θ)^T

The result follows by rearranging the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. □

Geometric Interpretation: The Cramér-Rao bound states that the covariance of any unbiased estimator is bounded below by the inverse Fisher metric applied to the gradient of the parameter function. This is precisely the statement that the “statistical distance” measured by the Fisher metric bounds the variance achievable by unbiased estimation.

Example 1.5 (Efficient Estimator). For the Gaussian location family with Fisher information I(μ) = 1, consider estimating τ(μ) = μ. We have ∇τ(μ) = 1, so the Cramér-Rao bound gives:

Var[T] ≥ 1² · 1^(-1) · 1 = 1

The sample mean T(X) = X achieves this bound exactly, making it an efficient estimator.

1.5 1.5 Jeffreys Prior and the Volume Element

The Fisher metric induces a natural volume form on the statistical manifold, leading to Jeffreys’ invariant prior.

Definition 1.10 (Riemannian Volume Form). The volume form associated with the Fisher metric is: dvol = √det(I(θ)) dθ₁ ∧ … ∧ dθₙ

Definition 1.11 (Jeffreys Prior). The Jeffreys prior is the probability measure on parameter space proportional to the Riemannian volume element: π_J(θ) ∝ √det(I(θ))

This prior has the crucial property of reparameterization invariance.

Theorem 1.12 (Invariance of Jeffreys Prior). If φ = h(θ) is a smooth reparameterization with Jacobian J = ∂h/∂θ, then the Jeffreys prior in the φ-parameterization is: π_J(φ) ∝ √det(I_φ(φ)) where I_φ(φ) = J^(-T) I(h^(-1)(φ)) J^(-1) is the Fisher information in the new coordinates.

Proof. Under reparameterization, the Fisher information transforms as: I_φ(φ) = J^(-T) I(θ) J^(-1)

Therefore: det(I_φ(φ)) = det(J^(-T)) det(I(θ)) det(J^(-1)) = det(I(θ))/det(J)²

The volume element transforms as: √det(I_φ(φ)) dφ = √det(I(θ))/|det(J)| |det(J)| dθ = √det(I(θ)) dθ

showing the invariance of the Jeffreys prior. □

Example 1.6 (Jeffreys Prior for Scale Parameter). Consider the exponential family p(x; σ) = σ^(-1) exp(-x/σ) for x > 0, σ > 0. The log-likelihood is:

ℓ(σ; x) = -log σ - x/σ

The score function is: s(σ; x) = -1/σ + x/σ²

The Fisher information is: I(σ) = E_σ[(1/σ - X/σ²)²] = E_σ[(1 - X/σ)²/σ²] = 1/σ²

The Jeffreys prior is: π_J(σ) ∝ √(1/σ²) = 1/σ

This is the standard noninformative prior for scale parameters.

1.6 1.6 Computational Implementation in Julia

We demonstrate computation of Fisher information and related quantities using Julia’s symbolic computation capabilities.

using Symbolics, LinearAlgebra, Distributions, ForwardDiff

# Define a parametric family symbolically
@variables θ[1:2] x

# Example: Bivariate normal with mean θ and identity covariance
function log_likelihood_symbolic(θ, x)
    -0.5 * sum((x .- θ).^2) - log(2π)
end

# Compute score function symbolically
function score_function(log_lik, θ, x)
    return Symbolics.gradient(log_lik(θ, x), θ)
end

# Compute Fisher information matrix
function fisher_information_symbolic(log_lik, θ, x)
    score = score_function(log_lik, θ, x)
    # For exponential families, Fisher info = -Hessian of log-likelihood
    H = Symbolics.hessian(log_lik(θ, x), θ)
    return -H  # This gives the Fisher information for this example
end

# Example computation
@variables μ[1:2] y[1:2]
LL(μ, y) = -0.5 * sum((y .- μ).^2)

# Compute symbolically
score = score_function(LL, μ, y)
fisher = fisher_information_symbolic(LL, μ, y)

println("Score function: ", score)
println("Fisher information: ", fisher)

For numerical computation of Fisher information from data:

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, Statistics

# Fisher information for multivariate normal location family
function fisher_info_mvnormal_location(d::Int)
    return Matrix(I, d, d)  # Identity matrix for unit covariance
end

# Fisher information for multinomial distribution
function fisher_info_multinomial(p::Vector{Float64})
    k = length(p)
    I_inv = diagm(p)  # Diagonal matrix of probabilities
    # Add rank-1 update for constraint Σp = 1
    return inv(I_inv + p * p')
end

# Example: Compute Fisher information for mixture of Gaussians
function mixture_gaussian_fisher_info(weights::Vector{Float64}, 
                                    means::Vector{Float64}, 
                                    vars::Vector{Float64})
    # Approximate Fisher information using observed information
    # This is more complex for mixture models - simplified version shown
    k = length(weights)
    n_params = 3 * k - 1  # weights (k-1) + means (k) + variances (k)
    
    # Placeholder computation - full implementation requires 
    # computation of expected second derivatives
    return Matrix(I, n_params, n_params)
end

# Demonstrate usage
p_multinomial = [0.3, 0.4, 0.3]
I_multi = fisher_info_multinomial(p_multinomial)
println("Multinomial Fisher Information:")
display(I_multi)

# Jeffreys prior computation
jeffreys_prior_density(I) = sqrt(det(I))

println("\nJeffreys prior density: ", jeffreys_prior_density(I_multi))

1.7 1.7 Examples of Statistical Manifolds

1.7.1 1.7.1 The Gaussian Manifold

The family of multivariate Gaussian distributions N(μ, Σ) with μ ∈ ℝ^d and Σ positive definite forms a statistical manifold of dimension d + d(d+1)/2.

Example 1.7 (Gaussian Location-Scale Family). Consider N(μ, σ²I) with parameters (μ, σ) ∈ ℝ^d × ℝ₊. The log-likelihood is:

ℓ(μ, σ; x) = -d/2 log(2π) - d log σ - (1/2σ²)||x - μ||²

The score function components are: - ∂ℓ/∂μ = (x - μ)/σ² - ∂ℓ/∂σ = -d/σ + ||x - μ||²/σ³

The Fisher information matrix is:

I(μ, σ) = [σ⁻²I_d    0    ]
          [0      2d/σ²]

This block-diagonal structure reflects the orthogonality between location and scale estimation.

1.7.2 1.7.2 Exponential Family Manifolds

Exponential families have particularly elegant geometric structure due to their canonical parameterization.

Definition 1.13 (Exponential Family). A k-parameter exponential family has density: p(x; θ) = exp(θ^T T(x) - A(θ) + h(x)) where T(x) are sufficient statistics, A(θ) is the cumulant generating function, and h(x) depends only on x.

For exponential families, the Fisher information has a simple form:

Theorem 1.14. For an exponential family, the Fisher information matrix is: I(θ) = ∇²A(θ) = Cov_θ[T(X)]

Proof. The score function is: s(θ; x) = T(x) - ∇A(θ) = T(x) - E_θ[T(X)]

Therefore: I(θ) = E_θ[s(θ; X)s(θ; X)^T] = Cov_θ[T(X)] = E_θ[(T(X) - E_θ[T(X)])(T(X) - E_θ[T(X)])^T]

Since A(θ) = log ∫ exp(θ^T T(x) + h(x)) dμ(x), we have: ∇A(θ) = E_θ[T(X)] ∇²A(θ) = Cov_θ[T(X)] = I(θ) □

Example 1.8 (Poisson Family). The Poisson distribution p(x; λ) = λ^x e^(-λ)/x! is exponential family with: - θ = log λ - T(x) = x
- A(θ) = e^θ = λ

The Fisher information is: I(λ) = d²A/dθ² · (dθ/dλ)² = λ · (1/λ)² = 1/λ

using Distributions, SpecialFunctions

# Compute Fisher information for common distributions
function fisher_info_poisson(λ::Float64)
    return 1/λ
end

function fisher_info_gamma(α::Float64, β::Float64)
    # For Gamma(α, β) parameterized with shape α and rate β
    ψ₁ = polygamma(1, α)  # Trigamma function
    return [ψ₁  -1/β; -1/β  α/β²]
end

function fisher_info_beta(α::Float64, β::Float64)
    ψ₁α = polygamma(1, α)
    ψ₁β = polygamma(1, β)
    ψ₁αβ = polygamma(1, α + β)
    
    return [ψ₁α - ψ₁αβ     -ψ₁αβ;
            -ψ₁αβ         ψ₁β - ψ₁αβ]
end

# Example computations
λ = 2.0
println("Poisson(λ=$λ) Fisher Information: ", fisher_info_poisson(λ))

α, β = 2.0, 3.0
println("Gamma(α=$α, β=$β) Fisher Information:")
display(fisher_info_gamma(α, β))

println("Beta(α=$α, β=$β) Fisher Information:")  
display(fisher_info_beta(α, β))

1.8 1.8 Geodesics and Statistical Distance

The Fisher metric defines geodesics on the statistical manifold, representing paths of maximal efficiency for statistical comparison.

Definition 1.15 (Statistical Distance). The Fisher-Rao distance between two distributions P_θ₁ and P_θ₂ is: d_FR(θ₁, θ₂) = inf_{γ} ∫₀¹ √(γ’(t)^T I(γ(t)) γ’(t)) dt where the infimum is over smooth paths γ:[0,1] → M with γ(0) = θ₁ and γ(1) = θ₂.

For the multinomial simplex, this distance has a closed form.

Theorem 1.16 (Fisher-Rao Distance on Simplex). For two points p, q on the multinomial simplex, the Fisher-Rao distance is: d_FR(p, q) = 2 arccos(Σᵢ √(pᵢqᵢ))

This is related to the Bhattacharyya coefficient BC(p,q) = Σᵢ √(pᵢqᵢ).

1.9 Exercises

  1. Fisher Information Computation: For the Weibull distribution with density p(x; α, β) = (α/β)(x/β)^(α-1) exp(-(x/β)^α), compute the Fisher information matrix I(α, β). Implement your calculation in Julia and verify numerically.

  2. Invariance Properties: Prove that the Fisher-Rao distance is invariant under smooth reparameterizations. That is, if φ = h(θ) is a diffeomorphism, show that d_FR(θ₁, θ₂) = d_FR(h(θ₁), h(θ₂)).

  3. Cramér-Rao Bound Application: Consider estimating the variance σ² of a normal distribution N(0, σ²) based on n i.i.d. observations. Find the Fisher information for σ², compute the Cramér-Rao bound for unbiased estimation, and verify that the sample variance achieves this bound asymptotically.

  4. Computational Implementation: Implement a Julia function that computes the Fisher information matrix numerically for any parametric family given by a log-likelihood function. Test it on the multivariate normal distribution N(μ, Σ) where both μ and Σ are unknown.

  5. Jeffreys Prior Computation: For the inverse Gamma distribution IG(α, β) with density p(x; α, β) ∝ x^(-α-1) exp(-β/x), derive the Jeffreys prior π_J(α, β). Implement the computation in Julia and create a contour plot.

  6. Geodesic Computation: On the 2-simplex {(p₁, p₂, p₃) : pᵢ > 0, Σpᵢ = 1}, compute the geodesic connecting (1/2, 1/4, 1/4) to (1/3, 1/3, 1/3) under the Fisher metric. Use numerical integration to find the Fisher-Rao distance.

  7. Exponential Family Theory: Prove that for a regular exponential family, the Fisher information matrix I(θ) = ∇²A(θ) is always positive definite on the interior of the natural parameter space. What happens at the boundary?

  8. Mixture Model Geometry: Consider the two-component Gaussian mixture p(x) = πφ(x; μ₁, σ₁) + (1-π)φ(x; μ₂, σ₂) where φ(·; μ, σ) is the normal density. Compute the Fisher information matrix with respect to the parameters (π, μ₁, μ₂, σ₁, σ₂). Implement this in Julia and study the conditioning of the Fisher information matrix as the components become nearly identical.

  9. Connection to Differential Geometry: Show that the Christoffel symbols for the Fisher metric on a regular exponential family are given by Γᵢⱼᵏ = E[∂³log p/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ∂θₖ]. Implement this for the Beta distribution and compare with symbolic computation.

  10. Advanced Theory: Prove that the Fisher metric is the unique Riemannian metric (up to scaling) on a statistical manifold that remains invariant under sufficient statistics. That is, if T(X) is sufficient for θ, then the Fisher information computed from p(T(x); θ) equals that from p(x; θ).

1.10 Connections

The geometric foundation established in this chapter underlies all subsequent developments in information geometry. Chapter 2 will explore how exponential families and mixture families have particularly elegant geometric structures, with dually flat manifolds and natural coordinate systems. The α-connections studied in Chapter 3 generalize the Levi-Civita connection of the Fisher metric, providing a one-parameter family of affine connections that unify statistical inference and differential geometry. The divergences in Chapter 4, particularly the Kullback-Leibler divergence, emerge as potential functions for these connections, connecting information theory to differential geometry through the framework established here.

The Fisher metric’s role as the natural Riemannian structure for statistical inference extends beyond pure mathematics into machine learning optimization (Chapter 5), where natural gradient descent uses the Fisher metric to define steepest descent directions in parameter space. Applications in thermodynamics (Chapter 7) reveal that the Fisher metric appears naturally in the geometry of equilibrium states, while neuroscience and quantum information applications (Chapter 8) demonstrate how information geometry provides universal principles governing information processing systems.

1.11 References

Amari, S. (1985). Differential-Geometrical Methods in Statistics. Springer-Verlag.

Amari, S., & Nagaoka, H. (2000). Methods of Information Geometry. American Mathematical Society.

Barndorff-Nielsen, O. E. (1978). Information and exponential families in statistical theory. Wiley Series in Probability and Mathematical Statistics.

Chentsov, N. N. (1982). Statistical Decision Rules and Optimal Inference. American Mathematical Society.

Efron, B. (1975). Defining the curvature of a statistical problem (with applications to second order efficiency). The Annals of Statistics, 3(6), 1189-1242.

Fisher, R. A. (1925). Theory of statistical estimation. Mathematical Proceedings of the Cambridge Philosophical Society, 22(5), 700-725.

Jeffreys, H. (1946). An invariant form for the prior probability in estimation problems. Proceedings of the Royal Society of London, 186(1007), 453-461.

Kass, R. E., & Vos, P. W. (1997). Geometrical Foundations of Asymptotic Inference. Wiley.

Murray, M. K., & Rice, J. W. (1993). Differential Geometry and Statistics. Chapman and Hall.

Rao, C. R. (1945). Information and accuracy attainable in the estimation of statistical parameters. Bulletin of the Calcutta Mathematical Society, 37, 81-91.

2 Chapter 2: Exponential and Mixture Families

The geometry of statistical models becomes particularly elegant when we focus on two fundamental classes of distributions: exponential families and mixture families. These families exhibit beautiful dualilty properties that illuminate the deep connections between statistical inference, optimization, and thermodynamics. The exponential family serves as the natural home for many important distributions in statistics, while mixture families provide the foundation for understanding convex combinations of probability measures. Together, they reveal how the Fisher information geometry partitions statistical manifolds into flat submanifolds with complementary curvature properties.

2.1 2.1 Exponential Families: Natural and Expectation Parameters

An exponential family represents one of the most important classes of probability distributions in mathematical statistics. These families possess remarkable geometric properties that make them the foundation for much of modern statistical theory.

Definition 2.1 (Exponential Family). A family of probability distributions {p(x; θ) : θ ∈ Θ} forms an exponential family if the probability density (or mass) function can be written as

p(x; θ) = exp(θᵀT(x) - ψ(θ)) h(x)

where θ ∈ ℝᵈ is the natural parameter vector, T(x) ∈ ℝᵈ is the sufficient statistic vector, ψ(θ) is the cumulant function (log partition function), and h(x) ≥ 0 is the base measure.

The natural parameter θ provides one coordinate system for the exponential family. The cumulant function ψ(θ) ensures proper normalization:

ψ(θ) = log ∫ exp(θᵀT(x)) h(x) dx

The geometric significance of exponential families becomes apparent when we consider the dual coordinate system given by the expectation parameters.

Definition 2.2 (Expectation Parameters). For an exponential family with natural parameters θ, the expectation parameters η ∈ ℝᵈ are defined as

η = ∇ψ(θ) = 𝔼θ[T(X)]

where the expectation is taken with respect to the distribution p(x; θ).

This transformation from θ to η represents a coordinate change on the statistical manifold. The relationship between these coordinate systems exhibits the structure of a Legendre transformation, a concept fundamental to classical mechanics and thermodynamics.

Theorem 2.1 (Legendre Duality for Exponential Families). The cumulant function ψ(θ) and the entropy function φ(η) = maxθ(θᵀη - ψ(θ)) form a Legendre dual pair. Specifically: 1. η = ∇ψ(θ) and θ = ∇φ(η) 2. ψ(θ) + φ(η) = θᵀη 3. Both ψ and φ are strictly convex functions

Proof. The first relation follows directly from the definition of expectation parameters. For the second relation, consider that φ(η) represents the conjugate function of ψ. By the envelope theorem and the first-order condition for the maximum in the definition of φ(η), we have η = ∇ψ(θ) where θ achieves the maximum. Substituting back yields φ(η) = θᵀη - ψ(θ), which gives the desired relation. The strict convexity of ψ follows from the fact that the Hessian ∇²ψ(θ) equals the covariance matrix of T(X), which is positive definite for non-degenerate exponential families. Strict convexity of φ follows by properties of Legendre transforms. □

The geometric interpretation is profound: the natural parameters θ provide a coordinate system where the exponential family manifold appears “curved,” while the expectation parameters η provide coordinates where the same manifold appears “flat” in a specific sense we will make precise shortly.

2.1.1 2.1.1 Examples of Exponential Families

Let us examine several fundamental exponential families to build intuition.

Example 2.1 (Gaussian Distribution). The univariate Gaussian distribution N(μ, σ²) can be written in exponential family form as:

p(x; μ, σ²) = exp(μx/σ² - x²/(2σ²) - μ²/(2σ²) - ½log(2πσ²))

Setting θ₁ = μ/σ² and θ₂ = -1/(2σ²), we obtain the natural parameterization with T(x) = [x, x²]ᵀ. The cumulant function is:

ψ(θ₁, θ₂) = -θ₁²/(4θ₂) - ½log(-2θ₂) - ½log(2π)

The expectation parameters are η₁ = 𝔼[X] = μ and η₂ = 𝔼[X²] = μ² + σ².

Example 2.2 (Multinomial Distribution). The multinomial distribution on k categories with probabilities p₁, …, pₖ (where pₖ = 1 - p₁ - … - pₖ₋₁) forms an exponential family with natural parameters θᵢ = log(pᵢ/pₖ) for i = 1, …, k-1. The sufficient statistics are the category counts T(x) = [x₁, …, xₖ₋₁]ᵀ.

These examples illustrate how the exponential family framework unifies seemingly disparate distributions under a common geometric structure.

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff
using Plots

# Demonstration of Legendre duality for the Gaussian case
function gaussian_cumulant(theta)
    # theta = [theta1, theta2] where theta1 = mu/sigma^2, theta2 = -1/(2*sigma^2)
    theta1, theta2 = theta
    return -theta1^2 / (4 * theta2) - 0.5 * log(-2 * theta2) - 0.5 * log(2π)
end

function gaussian_entropy(eta)
    # eta = [mu, mu^2 + sigma^2] = [eta1, eta2]
    eta1, eta2 = eta
    sigma2 = eta2 - eta1^2
    return 0.5 * log(2π * ℯ * sigma2)
end

# Verify Legendre duality: psi(theta) + phi(eta) = theta^T * eta
theta = [1.0, -0.5]  # corresponds to mu=2, sigma^2=1
eta = ForwardDiff.gradient(gaussian_cumulant, theta)
println("Expectation parameters η = ", eta)

psi_val = gaussian_cumulant(theta)
phi_val = gaussian_entropy(eta)
duality_check = psi_val + phi_val - dot(theta, eta)
println("Legendre duality check (should be ≈ 0): ", duality_check)

# Visualize the relationship between natural and expectation parameters
mu_range = -2:0.1:2
sigma_range = 0.5:0.1:2
theta1_vals = []
theta2_vals = []
eta1_vals = []
eta2_vals = []

for mu in mu_range, sigma in sigma_range
    theta1 = mu / sigma^2
    theta2 = -1 / (2 * sigma^2)
    eta1 = mu
    eta2 = mu^2 + sigma^2
    
    push!(theta1_vals, theta1)
    push!(theta2_vals, theta2)
    push!(eta1_vals, eta1)
    push!(eta2_vals, eta2)
end

# [FIGURE: Scatter plot showing the mapping between natural parameters (theta1, theta2) 
# and expectation parameters (eta1, eta2) for the Gaussian family, illustrating the 
# curved nature of the parameter transformation]

2.2 2.2 Mixture Families and Convex Geometry

While exponential families capture the essence of “exponential” curvature in statistical manifolds, mixture families represent the complementary notion of “mixture” geometry through convex combinations of probability distributions.

Definition 2.3 (Mixture Family). Let {p₁(x), p₂(x), …, pₖ(x)} be a collection of probability density functions. The mixture family M generated by these base distributions is:

M = {p(x; λ) = λ₁p₁(x) + λ₂p₂(x) + … + λₖpₖ(x) : λᵢ ≥ 0, Σᵢλᵢ = 1}

where λ = (λ₁, …, λₖ) are the mixing weights.

Mixture families have a natural geometric interpretation: they form convex combinations of probability measures, creating a simplex structure in the space of distributions. This convexity leads to fundamentally different geometric properties compared to exponential families.

The key insight is that exponential and mixture families represent orthogonal types of “flatness” in the geometry of statistical manifolds. This orthogonality manifests through the concepts of e-flat and m-flat submanifolds.

2.2.1 2.2.1 e-Flat and m-Flat Submanifolds

The Fisher information metric induces a Riemannian structure on statistical manifolds, but this metric admits special families of submanifolds that are “flat” with respect to specific affine connections.

Definition 2.4 (e-Flat Submanifold). A submanifold S of a statistical manifold is called e-flat (exponentially flat) if it can be parameterized such that the log-likelihood functions ℓ(x; θ) = log p(x; θ) are affine in the parameters θ.

Definition 2.5 (m-Flat Submanifold). A submanifold S of a statistical manifold is called m-flat (mixturally flat) if it can be parameterized such that the probability density functions p(x; λ) are affine in the parameters λ.

These definitions capture a fundamental duality in information geometry:

Theorem 2.2 (Exponential and Mixture Family Flatness). 1. Exponential families are e-flat submanifolds with respect to the e-connection. 2. Mixture families are m-flat submanifolds with respect to the m-connection. 3. e-flat and m-flat submanifolds are orthogonal with respect to the Fisher information metric.

Proof. For part 1, in an exponential family p(x; θ) = exp(θᵀT(x) - ψ(θ))h(x), the log-likelihood is ℓ(x; θ) = θᵀT(x) - ψ(θ) + log h(x), which is affine in θ after subtracting the data-independent terms. Part 2 follows from the definition of mixture families where p(x; λ) = Σᵢλᵢpᵢ(x) is manifestly affine in λ. Part 3 requires the theory of α-connections, which we defer to Chapter 3, but the intuition is that the “curved” directions of exponential families are orthogonal to the “curved” directions of mixture families. □

2.2.2 2.2.2 Geometric Projections

The orthogonality between e-flat and m-flat families leads to powerful geometric interpretations of fundamental statistical procedures.

Definition 2.6 (e-Projection and m-Projection). Let S be an e-flat submanifold and M be an m-flat submanifold of a statistical manifold. For any distribution q: 1. The e-projection of q onto M is the distribution p* ∈ M that minimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence KL(q ∥ p). 2. The m-projection of q onto S is the distribution p* ∈ S that minimizes the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence KL(p ∥ q).

These projections have profound statistical interpretations:

Theorem 2.3 (Projections and Statistical Inference). 1. Maximum likelihood estimation corresponds to m-projection of the empirical distribution onto the model family. 2. Maximum entropy estimation corresponds to e-projection subject to moment constraints.

Proof. For part 1, let p̂(x) = n⁻¹Σⁿᵢ₌₁δ(x - xᵢ) be the empirical distribution. The maximum likelihood estimator minimizes:

KL(p̂ ∥ pθ) = ∫ p̂(x) log(p̂(x)/pθ(x)) dx = -∫ p̂(x) log pθ(x) dx + const.

This is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood, establishing the connection to m-projection.

For part 2, maximum entropy with moment constraints seeks to maximize entropy subject to 𝔼p[Tᵢ(X)] = cᵢ. The Lagrangian approach shows that the solution has the exponential family form p*(x) ∝ exp(ΣᵢλᵢTᵢ(x)), which corresponds to e-projection onto the constraint set. □

2.3 2.3 Pythagoras-Like Theorems in Information Geometry

One of the most elegant results in information geometry is the generalization of the Pythagorean theorem to statistical manifolds. This theorem provides a geometric interpretation of many statistical procedures.

Theorem 2.4 (Information Geometric Pythagorean Theorem). Let S be an e-flat submanifold, M be an m-flat submanifold, and let q be any distribution. If p* is the e-projection of q onto M and r* is the m-projection of q onto S, then:

KL(r* ∥ p) + KL(p ∥ q) = KL(r* ∥ q)

Proof. The key insight is that the Fisher information metric induces an orthogonal decomposition similar to Euclidean geometry. Using the properties of e-flat and m-flat submanifolds and the definitions of the projections, we can show that the “information geometric angle” between the paths from r* to p* and from p* to q is π/2, leading to the additive property of divergences analogous to the Pythagorean theorem in Euclidean space. The detailed proof requires the machinery of α-connections, which we develop in Chapter 3. □

This theorem has immediate applications to understanding the geometry of statistical estimation:

Example 2.3 (Maximum Likelihood and Maximum Entropy Duality). Consider estimating the parameters of an exponential family from data. Let q be the true distribution and p̂ be the empirical distribution. The maximum likelihood estimator p*_ML and the maximum entropy estimator p*_ME satisfy:

KL(p*_ME ∥ p*_ML) + KL(p*_ML ∥ p̂) = KL(p*_ME ∥ p̂)

This shows that the maximum likelihood estimator lies “between” the empirical distribution and the maximum entropy estimator in information geometric terms.

using Distributions, Optim, StatsBase

# Demonstration of e-projection and m-projection for a simple exponential family
struct BernoulliFamily
    # Natural parameterization: p(x; theta) = exp(theta * x - psi(theta))
    # where psi(theta) = log(1 + exp(theta))
end

function cumulant_function(theta)
    return log(1 + exp(theta))
end

function natural_to_expectation(theta)
    return exp(theta) / (1 + exp(theta))
end

function expectation_to_natural(eta)
    return log(eta / (1 - eta))
end

# Generate some data from a Bernoulli(0.3) distribution
Random.seed!(42)
true_p = 0.3
n_samples = 100
data = rand(Bernoulli(true_p), n_samples)
empirical_mean = mean(data)

println("True parameter: p = ", true_p)
println("Empirical mean: ", empirical_mean)

# Maximum likelihood estimation (m-projection)
mle_p = empirical_mean  # For Bernoulli, MLE is simply the sample mean
mle_theta = expectation_to_natural(mle_p)
println("MLE: p̂ = ", mle_p, ", θ̂ = ", mle_theta)

# Maximum entropy estimation with mean constraint (e-projection)
# This coincides with MLE for exponential families
maxent_theta = expectation_to_natural(empirical_mean)
maxent_p = natural_to_expectation(maxent_theta)
println("MaxEnt: p = ", maxent_p, ", θ = ", maxent_theta)

# Verify Pythagorean theorem using KL divergences
function kl_bernoulli(p, q)
    if p ≈ 0
        return q ≈ 0 ? 0.0 : Inf
    elseif p ≈ 1
        return q ≈ 1 ? 0.0 : Inf
    else
        return p * log(p/q) + (1-p) * log((1-p)/(1-q))
    end
end

# For this simple case, MLE and MaxEnt coincide, so the theorem becomes trivial
# But we can verify the structure for a more complex mixture example

# Create a mixture of two Bernoulli distributions
p1, p2 = 0.2, 0.8
mixing_weight = 0.4
mixture_data = []
for i in 1:n_samples
    if rand() < mixing_weight
        push!(mixture_data, rand(Bernoulli(p1)))
    else
        push!(mixture_data, rand(Bernoulli(p2)))
    end
end

mixture_mean = mean(mixture_data)
println("\nMixture model:")
println("True mixture mean: ", mixing_weight * p1 + (1-mixing_weight) * p2)
println("Empirical mixture mean: ", mixture_mean)

# [FIGURE: Visualization of the information geometric relationships showing 
# e-projections and m-projections on the statistical manifold, with the 
# Pythagorean relationship between KL divergences]

2.4 2.4 Applications to Maximum Likelihood and Maximum Entropy

The geometric framework of exponential and mixture families provides profound insights into two fundamental principles of statistical inference: maximum likelihood estimation and maximum entropy estimation. These principles, which appear quite different from an algorithmic perspective, are revealed to be dual geometric operations in information geometry.

2.4.1 2.4.1 Maximum Likelihood as m-Projection

Maximum likelihood estimation finds the parameter values that make the observed data most probable under the assumed model. In the language of information geometry, this corresponds to finding the point in the model family that is closest to the empirical distribution in terms of KL divergence.

Theorem 2.5 (Maximum Likelihood as m-Projection). Let X₁, …, Xₙ be i.i.d. samples from an unknown distribution, and let p̂ₙ(x) = n⁻¹Σⁿᵢ₌₁δ(x - Xᵢ) be the empirical distribution. For a parametric family {pθ : θ ∈ Θ}, the maximum likelihood estimator θ̂ satisfies:

pθ̂ = argminp∈{pθ:θ∈Θ} KL(p̂ₙ ∥ p)

Proof. The KL divergence between the empirical distribution and the model is:

KL(p̂ₙ ∥ pθ) = ∫ p̂ₙ(x) log(p̂ₙ(x)/pθ(x)) dx = -∫ p̂ₙ(x) log pθ(x) dx + H(p̂ₙ)

Since H(p̂ₙ) is independent of θ, minimizing KL(p̂ₙ ∥ pθ) is equivalent to maximizing ∫ p̂ₙ(x) log pθ(x) dx = n⁻¹Σⁿᵢ₌₁log pθ(Xᵢ), which is precisely the log-likelihood function. □

This result shows that maximum likelihood estimation can be interpreted as projecting the empirical distribution onto the model family using the “forward” KL divergence. The geometric perspective reveals why maximum likelihood enjoys certain optimality properties: it finds the point in the model family that is closest to the data in a natural information-theoretic sense.

2.4.2 2.4.2 Maximum Entropy as e-Projection

Maximum entropy estimation, originally developed by Jaynes for statistical mechanics, seeks the distribution that maximizes entropy subject to constraints. This principle has found widespread applications in machine learning, natural language processing, and statistical physics.

Theorem 2.6 (Maximum Entropy as e-Projection). Let C = {p : ∫ p(x)Tᵢ(x)dx = cᵢ, i = 1,…,k} be a constraint set defined by moment conditions. The maximum entropy distribution p* satisfies:

p* = argminp∈C KL(p ∥ p₀)

where p₀ is a reference distribution (often uniform).

Proof. Using Lagrange multipliers, the constrained optimization problem becomes:

maximize H(p) = -∫ p(x) log p(x) dx subject to ∫ p(x)Tᵢ(x) dx = cᵢ and ∫ p(x) dx = 1

The Lagrangian is L = -∫ p(x) log p(x) dx - λ₀(∫ p(x) dx - 1) - Σᵢλᵢ(∫ p(x)Tᵢ(x) dx - cᵢ).

Taking the functional derivative with respect to p and setting it to zero yields: -log p*(x) - 1 - λ₀ - ΣᵢλᵢTᵢ(x) = 0

This gives p*(x) = exp(-1 - λ₀ - ΣᵢλᵢTᵢ(x)), which has the exponential family form. The connection to e-projection follows from the dual formulation of the maximum entropy problem. □

The maximum entropy principle thus corresponds to e-projection onto the constraint set, using the “reverse” KL divergence. This geometric interpretation explains why maximum entropy distributions have the exponential family form and why they interpolate smoothly between known constraints.

Example 2.4 (Maximum Entropy for Discrete Distributions). Consider estimating a discrete distribution over {1, 2, …, k} subject to the constraint that the expected value is μ. The maximum entropy solution is:

p*(i) ∝ exp(λi)

where λ is chosen so that Σᵢip*(i) = μ. This gives a geometric distribution when μ > (k+1)/2 and interpolates between uniform and concentrated distributions as μ varies.

using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL
using Distributions, StatsBase

# Maximum entropy estimation for discrete distribution with moment constraints
function maxent_discrete(support, moment_constraints, reference_weights = nothing)
    k = length(support)
    if reference_weights === nothing
        reference_weights = ones(k) / k  # Uniform reference
    end
    
    # Objective function: negative entropy plus constraint violations
    function objective(λ, p)
        # Compute probabilities from Lagrange multipliers
        log_probs = -sum(λ[i] * support .* moment_constraints[i] for i in 1:length(λ))
        probs = exp.(log_probs .- maximum(log_probs))
        probs ./= sum(probs)
        
        # Negative entropy (we minimize, so negate)
        entropy = -sum(p * log(p + 1e-12) for p in probs)
        
        # Constraint violations (as penalties)
        violations = sum(abs(sum(support .* probs) - moment_constraints[i])^2 
                        for i in 1:length(moment_constraints))
        
        return -entropy + 1000 * violations, probs
    end
    
    # Optimize using Lagrange multipliers
    λ_init = zeros(length(moment_constraints))
    
    result = optimize(λ -> objective(λ, reference_weights)[1], λ_init, BFGS())
    λ_opt = result.minimizer
    
    # Compute final probabilities
    _, probs_opt = objective(λ_opt, reference_weights)
    return probs_opt, λ_opt
end

# Example: Maximum entropy distribution on {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} with mean = 3.5
support = 1:5
target_mean = 3.5
probs_maxent, λ_opt = maxent_discrete(support, [target_mean])

println("Maximum entropy distribution:")
for (i, p) in enumerate(probs_maxent)
    println("P(X = $i) = $(round(p, digits=4))")
end
println("Achieved mean: ", sum(support .* probs_maxent))
println("Entropy: ", -sum(p * log(p + 1e-12) for p in probs_maxent))

# Compare with uniform distribution (unconstrained maximum entropy)
uniform_probs = ones(5) / 5
uniform_entropy = -sum(p * log(p) for p in uniform_probs)
println("\nUniform distribution entropy: ", uniform_entropy)
println("Constrained entropy (should be lower): ", -sum(p * log(p + 1e-12) for p in probs_maxent))

# Demonstrate the e-projection interpretation
function kl_divergence(p, q)
    return sum(p[i] * log(p[i] / q[i]) for i in 1:length(p))
end

println("\nKL divergence from maxent to uniform: ", 
        kl_divergence(probs_maxent, uniform_probs))

# [FIGURE: Bar plots comparing uniform distribution, maximum entropy distribution 
# with mean constraint, and empirical distribution, showing how maximum entropy 
# interpolates between uniform and data-driven estimates]

2.4.3 2.4.3 Duality Between ML and MaxEnt

The geometric framework reveals a beautiful duality between maximum likelihood and maximum entropy estimation. While they operate on different sides of the KL divergence, they are connected through the orthogonality of e-flat and m-flat submanifolds.

Theorem 2.7 (ML-MaxEnt Duality). For an exponential family model with sufficient statistics T(x), the maximum likelihood estimator from data X₁, …, Xₙ coincides with the maximum entropy estimator subject to the moment constraints 𝔼[Tᵢ(X)] = n⁻¹Σⁿⱼ₌₁Tᵢ(Xⱼ).

Proof. In an exponential family, the log-likelihood is ℓ(θ) = Σⁿⱼ₌₁(θᵀT(Xⱼ) - ψ(θ)). Setting the gradient to zero gives:

∇ℓ(θ) = Σⁿⱼ₌₁T(Xⱼ) - n∇ψ(θ) = 0

This yields ∇ψ(θ̂) = n⁻¹Σⁿⱼ₌₁T(Xⱼ), which means the expected sufficient statistics under the MLE equal the empirical sufficient statistics.

The maximum entropy estimator with constraints 𝔼[Tᵢ(X)] = ĉᵢ has the form p*(x) ∝ exp(λᵀT(x)). The constraint equations give 𝔼[T(X)] = ∇ψ(λ) = ĉ. When ĉᵢ = n⁻¹Σⁿⱼ₌₁Tᵢ(Xⱼ), we obtain λ = θ̂, showing the methods coincide. □

This duality explains why exponential families are so fundamental in statistics: they are precisely the families where the two major principles of statistical inference—maximum likelihood and maximum entropy—yield identical results.

2.5 2.5 Advanced Topics: Curved Exponential Families

While our focus has been on standard exponential families, many important statistical models belong to curved exponential families, where the natural parameter space is constrained to a lower-dimensional submanifold.

Definition 2.7 (Curved Exponential Family). A curved exponential family is specified by p(x; ξ) = exp(θ(ξ)ᵀT(x) - ψ(θ(ξ)))h(x), where θ: Ξ → Θ is a smooth map from the model parameter space Ξ ⊂ ℝᵈ to the natural parameter space Θ ⊂ ℝᵏ, with d < k.

Example 2.5 (Multinomial with Constraints). Consider a multinomial distribution on three categories where the probabilities satisfy p₁ = p₂. This constraint defines a curved exponential family embedded in the full 2-dimensional multinomial family.

Curved exponential families inherit some but not all properties of standard exponential families. They remain e-flat in the full natural parameter space but appear curved when viewed in their intrinsic parameterization. This curvature has important implications for statistical inference and information geometric analysis.

# Example of curved exponential family: constrained multinomial
using LinearAlgebra, Optim

# Multinomial with constraint p1 = p2
struct ConstrainedMultinomial
    # Parameter ξ = [p1, p3] where p2 = p1 and p1 + p2 + p3 = 1
    # So p1 = p2 = ξ[1], p3 = ξ[2], and ξ[1] + ξ[1] + ξ[2] = 1
    # Therefore: p1 = p2 = (1 - ξ[2])/2, p3 = ξ[2]
end

function constrained_to_natural(xi)
    # xi = [xi2] where xi2 = p3
    # p1 = p2 = (1 - xi2)/2, p3 = xi2
    p3 = xi[1]
    p1 = p2 = (1 - p3) / 2
    
    # Natural parameters: theta_i = log(p_i / p3) for i = 1,2
    theta1 = log(p1 / p3)
    theta2 = log(p2 / p3)  # Same as theta1 due to constraint
    
    return [theta1, theta2]
end

function natural_to_expectation(theta)
    # eta_i = E[X_i] = p_i for multinomial
    exp_theta = exp.(theta)
    z = 1 + sum(exp_theta)  # Normalization
    
    p1 = exp_theta[1] / z
    p2 = exp_theta[2] / z  
    p3 = 1 / z
    
    return [p1, p2, p3]
end

# Generate data from constrained model
xi_true = [0.4]  # p3 = 0.4, so p1 = p2 = 0.3
p_true = [0.3, 0.3, 0.4]
n_trials = 1000
data = rand(Multinomial(1, p_true), n_trials)
counts = sum(data, dims=2)[:, 1]

println("True probabilities: ", p_true)
println("Observed counts: ", counts)
println("Empirical probabilities: ", counts / n_trials)

# MLE for constrained model
function log_likelihood(xi, counts)
    p3 = xi[1]
    p1 


\newpage

# Chapter 3: α-Connections and Duality

The geometric structure of statistical manifolds extends far beyond the Riemannian metric provided by the Fisher information. While the Fisher metric equips a statistical manifold with a unique Levi-Civita connection, information geometry reveals that there exists a rich family of affine connections, parameterized by a real number α, that capture different aspects of statistical inference and learning. These α-connections, introduced by Amari, provide a framework for understanding the duality between exponential and mixture representations of probability distributions, leading to powerful geometric insights into divergence measures and optimization algorithms.

## The Family of α-Connections

In Riemannian geometry, the metric tensor uniquely determines the Levi-Civita connection through the fundamental theorem of Riemannian geometry. However, on a statistical manifold, we can construct a one-parameter family of torsion-free affine connections that respect the underlying statistical structure while providing different geometric perspectives.

> **Definition 3.1 (α-Connection).** Let (S, g) be a statistical manifold with Fisher information metric g. The α-connection ∇^(α) is the unique torsion-free affine connection satisfying:
> 
> X · g(Y, Z) = g(∇^(α)_X Y, Z) + g(Y, ∇^(α*)_X Z)
> 
> where α* = -α is the dual parameter, and the connection coefficients are given by:
> 
> Γ^(α)_{ijk} = E_p[(∂_i ∂_j log p)(∂_k log p)] + (1-α)/2 · E_p[(∂_i log p)(∂_j log p)(∂_k log p)]

The parameter α controls the "skewness" of the connection. When α = 0, we recover the Levi-Civita connection of the Fisher metric. The cases α = 1 and α = -1 have special significance and deserve particular attention.

> **Definition 3.2 (Exponential and Mixture Connections).** The exponential connection (e-connection) corresponds to α = 1:
> 
> Γ^(1)_{ijk} = E_p[(∂_i ∂_j log p)(∂_k log p)]
> 
> The mixture connection (m-connection) corresponds to α = -1:
> 
> Γ^(-1)_{ijk} = E_p[(∂_i ∂_j log p)(∂_k log p)] + E_p[(∂_i log p)(∂_j log p)(∂_k log p)]

These connections encode fundamentally different ways of "transporting" probability distributions along curves in the statistical manifold. The e-connection is naturally adapted to exponential families, while the m-connection is suited to mixture families.

### Geometric Interpretation

The α-connections can be understood through the lens of generalized means. Consider a geodesic triangle with vertices representing three probability distributions. In Euclidean geometry, the "center" of such a triangle is naturally the arithmetic mean of the vertices. However, on a statistical manifold, different connections give rise to different notions of "center."

For α = 1 (e-connection), the geodesic interpolation between distributions p₁ and p₂ along parameter λ ∈ [0,1] takes the form:

p_λ(x) ∝ p₁(x)^(1-λ) p₂(x)^λ


For α = -1 (m-connection), the interpolation becomes:

p_λ(x) = (1-λ)p₁(x) + λp₂(x)


This geometric interpolation reveals the duality: e-geodesics correspond to geometric means of densities, while m-geodesics correspond to arithmetic means.

## Dually Flat Structure

The most remarkable property of α-connections is that they come in dual pairs, leading to the concept of dually flat manifolds. This structure is particularly elegant for exponential families.

> **Definition 3.3 (Dually Flat Manifold).** A statistical manifold (S, g, ∇^(α), ∇^(α*)) is called dually flat if both ∇^(α) and ∇^(α*) are flat (have zero curvature) and dual with respect to the metric g.

> **Theorem 3.1 (Dually Flat Structure of Exponential Families).** Every exponential family admits a dually flat structure with respect to the (1,-1)-connections. In natural coordinates (η₁,...,ηₙ) and expectation coordinates (μ₁,...,μₙ), the e-connection is flat in η-coordinates and the m-connection is flat in μ-coordinates.

*Proof.* Consider an exponential family with natural parameters η = (η₁,...,ηₙ) and sufficient statistics T(x) = (T₁(x),...,Tₙ(x)). The log-likelihood is:

ℓ(η; x) = η^T T(x) - ψ(η)


In η-coordinates, we have:

∂ᵢ∂ⱼℓ = -∂ᵢ∂ⱼψ = -gᵢⱼ ∂ᵢ∂ⱼ∂ₖℓ = -∂ᵢ∂ⱼ∂ₖψ


The e-connection coefficients become:

Γ^(1)ᵢⱼₖ = E[(∂ᵢ∂ⱼℓ)(∂ₖℓ)] = E[(-gᵢⱼ)(∂ₖℓ)] = 0


since E[∂ₖℓ] = 0. Thus the e-connection is flat in η-coordinates.

Similarly, in expectation coordinates μᵢ = E[Tᵢ(x)], the m-connection is flat. The coordinate transformation between η and μ is given by the Legendre transform μ = ∇ψ(η) and η = ∇φ(μ), where φ is the convex conjugate of ψ. □

This theorem reveals the profound connection between the geometry of exponential families and convex analysis. The dually flat structure means that exponential families can be parameterized by two coordinate systems—natural parameters and expectation parameters—such that geodesics are straight lines in each coordinate system for the appropriate connection.

### Computational Implementation

Let's implement the α-connections for a simple exponential family—the univariate Gaussian distribution—to illustrate these geometric concepts computationally.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff, Plots

struct GaussianManifold
    """Statistical manifold of univariate Gaussian distributions N(μ, σ²)"""
end

# Natural parameters: η₁ = μ/σ², η₂ = -1/(2σ²)
function natural_to_standard(η)
    η₁, η₂ = η
    σ² = -1/(2*η₂)
    μ = η₁ * σ²
    return [μ, σ²]
end

# Standard parameters to natural parameters
function standard_to_natural(θ)
    μ, σ² = θ
    η₁ = μ/σ²
    η₂ = -1/(2*σ²)
    return [η₁, η₂]
end

# Log-partition function
function log_partition(η)
    η₁, η₂ = η
    return -η₁²/(4*η₂) - 0.5*log(-2*η₂) - 0.5*log(2π)
end

# Fisher information matrix in natural coordinates
function fisher_information_natural(η)
    ψ_hess = ForwardDiff.hessian(log_partition, η)
    return ψ_hess
end

# α-connection coefficients
function alpha_connection_coefficients(η, α)
    # Third derivatives of log-partition function
    ψ_third = ForwardDiff.jacobian(x -> ForwardDiff.hessian(log_partition, x), η)
    
    # Reshape to get ∂³ψ/∂ηᵢ∂ηⱼ∂ηₖ
    n = length(η)
    coeffs = zeros(n, n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n, k in 1:n
        # For exponential families: Γ^(α)ᵢⱼₖ = (1+α)/2 * ∂³ψ/∂ηᵢ∂ηⱼ∂ηₖ
        coeffs[i,j,k] = (1+α)/2 * ψ_third[i, (j-1)*n + k]
    end
    
    return coeffs
end

# Example: e-connection (α=1) coefficients for N(0,1)
η₀ = standard_to_natural([0.0, 1.0])  # N(0,1) in natural coordinates
Γ_e = alpha_connection_coefficients(η₀, 1.0)
Γ_m = alpha_connection_coefficients(η₀, -1.0)

println("e-connection coefficients at N(0,1):")
println("Γ¹₁₁₁ = $(Γ_e[1,1,1])")
println("Γ¹₁₁₂ = $(Γ_e[1,1,2])")

println("\nm-connection coefficients at N(0,1):")
println("Γ⁻¹₁₁₁ = $(Γ_m[1,1,1])")
println("Γ⁻¹₁₁₂ = $(Γ_m[1,1,2])")

2.6 α-Divergences and Information Measures

The α-connections give rise to a family of divergence measures that generalize the Kullback-Leibler divergence. These α-divergences provide distance-like measures between probability distributions that respect the underlying geometric structure.

Definition 3.4 (α-Divergence). For two probability distributions p and q on the same sample space, the α-divergence is defined by:

D_α(p‖q) = (4/(1-α²)) ∫ [(1+α)/2 · p(x) + (1-α)/2 · q(x) - p(x)^((1+α)/2) q(x)^((1-α)/2)] dx

The parameter α controls the asymmetry of the divergence. Several important cases deserve special attention:

  • α → 0: D₀(p‖q) = KL(p‖q) + KL(q‖p), the symmetrized Kullback-Leibler divergence
  • α → 1: D₁(p‖q) = 2(1 - ∫ √p(x)q(x) dx), twice the Hellinger distance
  • α → -1: D₋₁(p‖q) = 2(∫ p(x)log(p(x)/q(x)) dx), twice the Kullback-Leibler divergence

Theorem 3.2 (Geometric Interpretation of α-Divergences). The α-divergence D_α(p‖q) equals the squared geodesic distance between p and q measured in the dually flat coordinate system associated with the α-connection.

This theorem establishes the fundamental connection between the statistical and geometric properties of α-divergences. In the natural coordinate system of an exponential family, the α = 1 divergence becomes a simple quadratic form, while in the expectation coordinate system, the α = -1 divergence takes this form.

2.6.1 Bregman Divergences

The α-divergences are closely related to Bregman divergences, which arise naturally from convex functions and provide a general framework for measuring discrepancy.

Definition 3.5 (Bregman Divergence). Given a strictly convex, differentiable function φ: Ω → ℝ, the Bregman divergence is:

B_φ(x‖y) = φ(x) - φ(y) - ⟨∇φ(y), x-y⟩

In the context of exponential families, the log-partition function ψ and its convex conjugate φ generate dual Bregman divergences that correspond exactly to the α = -1 and α = 1 divergences respectively.

Theorem 3.3 (Bregman-α Correspondence). For an exponential family with log-partition function ψ and its convex conjugate φ:

  1. D₋₁(p_η‖p_ξ) = B_ψ(η‖ξ) (divergence in natural coordinates)
  2. D₁(p_η‖p_ξ) = B_φ(μ_η‖μ_ξ) (divergence in expectation coordinates)

where μ_η = ∇ψ(η) are the expectation parameters.

This correspondence reveals why exponential families have such elegant geometric properties: their parameter spaces are organized by pairs of dual convex functions, leading to dual flat geometric structures.

2.6.2 Canonical Divergence

For dually flat manifolds, there exists a canonical divergence that captures the essential geometric structure.

Definition 3.6 (Canonical Divergence). On a dually flat manifold with dual coordinate systems (θ, η) related by θ = ∇ψ(η) and η = ∇φ(θ), the canonical divergence is:

D(p‖q) = ψ(η_p) + φ(θ_q) - ⟨η_p, θ_q⟩

This divergence is particularly elegant because it reduces to Bregman divergences in either coordinate system, unifying the geometric and information-theoretic perspectives.

# Implementation of canonical divergence for exponential families
function canonical_divergence(η_p, θ_q, ψ, φ)
    """
    Canonical divergence between distributions parameterized by
    η_p (natural) and θ_q (expectation coordinates)
    """
    return ψ(η_p) + φ(θ_q) - dot(η_p, θ_q)
end

# Example: canonical divergence for Gaussian family
function gaussian_psi(η)
    """Log-partition function for univariate Gaussian"""
    η₁, η₂ = η
    return -η₁²/(4*η₂) - 0.5*log(-2*η₂) - 0.5*log(2π)
end

function gaussian_phi(θ)
    """Convex conjugate (expectation coordinate potential)"""
    μ, σ² = θ
    return 0.5*μ²/σ² + 0.5*log(2π*σ²)
end

# Compare divergences between N(0,1) and N(1,4)
η_p = standard_to_natural([0.0, 1.0])  # N(0,1)
η_q = standard_to_natural([1.0, 4.0])  # N(1,4)
θ_q = [1.0, 4.0]  # expectation coordinates for N(1,4)

div_canonical = canonical_divergence(η_p, θ_q, gaussian_psi, gaussian_phi)
println("Canonical divergence D(N(0,1)‖N(1,4)) = $(div_canonical)")

# Verify against KL divergence formula
kl_analytical = 0.5 * (1/4 + (0-1)²/4 - 1 + log(4))
println("KL divergence (analytical) = $(kl_analytical)")
println("Difference = $(abs(div_canonical - kl_analytical))")

2.7 Dualistic Properties and Applications

The duality between α-connections leads to several important dualistic properties that have profound implications for statistical inference and machine learning.

2.7.1 Projection Theorems

One of the most powerful applications of α-geometry is the generalization of orthogonal projection to statistical manifolds.

Theorem 3.4 (Generalized Pythagorean Theorem). Let M be a dually flat submanifold of a statistical manifold S. For any point p ∈ S, let p* be its α-projection onto M and q be any other point in M. Then:

D_α(p‖q) = D_α(p‖p) + D_{-α}(p‖q)

This theorem generalizes the Pythagorean theorem to information geometry, providing a fundamental decomposition of divergences that is essential for understanding model selection, variational inference, and iterative scaling algorithms.

2.7.2 EM Algorithm and α-Geometry

The Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm can be understood as alternating α-projections between dual coordinate systems.

Theorem 3.5 (Geometric EM). The EM algorithm for exponential family models performs alternating projections: 1. E-step: α = -1 projection (mixture connection)
2. M-step: α = 1 projection (exponential connection)

This geometric interpretation explains why EM converges and provides insight into developing more general optimization algorithms using α-connections.

using Distributions, Random

function em_geometric_interpretation(data, n_components, max_iter=100)
    """
    EM algorithm for Gaussian mixture models with geometric interpretation
    """
    n, d = size(data)
    
    # Initialize parameters
    π = fill(1/n_components, n_components)
    μ = [mean(data, dims=1)[:] + randn(d) for _ in 1:n_components]
    Σ = [Matrix(1.0I, d, d) for _ in 1:n_components]
    
    log_likelihood_history = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # E-step: mixture connection projection
        # Compute responsibilities (posterior probabilities)
        γ = zeros(n, n_components)
        for i in 1:n, k in 1:n_components
            γ[i,k] = π[k] * pdf(MvNormal(μ[k], Σ[k]), data[i,:])
        end
        γ = γ ./ sum(γ, dims=2)  # Normalize
        
        # M-step: exponential connection projection  
        # Update parameters by projecting onto exponential family
        for k in 1:n_components
            # Update mixture weights
            N_k = sum(γ[:,k])
            π[k] = N_k / n
            
            # Update means
            μ[k] = sum(γ[i,k] * data[i,:] for i in 1:n) / N_k
            
            # Update covariances
            Σ[k] = sum(γ[i,k] * (data[i,:] - μ[k]) * (data[i,:] - μ[k])' for i in 1:n) / N_k
        end
        
        # Compute log-likelihood
        ll = sum(log(sum(π[k] * pdf(MvNormal(μ[k], Σ[k]), data[i,:]) 
                        for k in 1:n_components)) for i in 1:n)
        push!(log_likelihood_history, ll)
        
        # Check convergence
        if iter > 1 && abs(log_likelihood_history[end] - log_likelihood_history[end-1]) < 1e-6
            break
        end
    end
    
    return π, μ, Σ, log_likelihood_history
end

# Generate sample data
Random.seed!(42)
data = vcat(
    randn(50, 2) .+ [0.0 0.0]',   # First component
    randn(50, 2) .+ [3.0 3.0]'    # Second component  
)

# Run EM with geometric interpretation
π, μ, Σ, history = em_geometric_interpretation(data, 2)

println("Final mixture weights: π = $(round.(π, digits=3))")
println("Final means:")
for (i, m) in enumerate(μ)
    println("  μ[$i] = $(round.(m, digits=3))")
end

# Plot convergence
plot(history, xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="Log-Likelihood", 
     title="EM Convergence via α-Projections", lw=2)

2.8 Advanced Topics in α-Geometry

2.8.1 Higher-Order α-Connections

The framework of α-connections can be extended to incorporate higher-order statistical information, leading to deeper geometric structures.

Definition 3.7 (Higher-Order α-Connections). The (α,β)-connection incorporates both third and fourth-order cumulants:

Γ^(α,β){ijk} = E_p[(∂_i ∂_j log p)(∂_k log p)] + α/2 · E_p[(∂_i log p)(∂_j log p)(∂_k log p)] + β/6 · κ{ijk}

where κ_{ijk} is the third-order cumulant tensor.

This extension is particularly relevant for understanding non-exponential family models and robust statistical procedures.

2.8.2 Information Geometry of Composite Models

When dealing with composite statistical models—such as hierarchical models or models with both discrete and continuous components—the α-connection framework extends naturally.

Consider a composite model p(x,z|θ) where x is observed and z is latent. The marginal model p(x|θ) = ∫p(x,z|θ)dz induces its own geometric structure, and the relationship between the joint and marginal geometries is mediated by α-connections.

Theorem 3.6 (Marginal α-Geometry). If (X×Z, g^{(joint)}, ∇^{(α)}) is the joint statistical manifold, then the marginal manifold (X, g^{(marg)}, ∇^{(α,marg)}) has α-connection coefficients related to the joint connection through:

Γ^{(α,marg)}{ijk} = ∫ Γ^{(α,joint)}{ijk}(x,z) p(z|x,θ) dz + correction terms

The correction terms encode the additional curvature introduced by marginalization and are crucial for understanding the geometry of latent variable models.

2.9 Exercises

Exercise 3.1 (Computational). Implement the α-divergence for the exponential family of Poisson distributions. Compare D₁, D₀, and D₋₁ between Poisson(λ=2) and Poisson(λ=5). Verify that D₀ corresponds to the symmetrized KL divergence.

Exercise 3.2 (Theoretical). Prove that for any α ∈ ℝ, the α-connection ∇^(α) is torsion-free. Show that the curvature tensor of ∇^(α) can be expressed in terms of the fourth-order cumulants of the statistical model.

Exercise 3.3 (Computational). For the beta distribution family Beta(a,b), implement functions to: - Convert between (a,b) and natural parameters (η₁,η₂) - Compute the Fisher information matrix - Calculate α-connection coefficients - Visualize geodesics for α = -1, 0, 1 on the (a,b) parameter space

Exercise 3.4 (Theoretical). Consider the one-dimensional exponential family with density p(x|θ) = exp(θx - ψ(θ)). Prove that: - The e-connection is flat in the θ-coordinate
- The m-connection is flat in the μ = ψ’(θ) coordinate - The transformation μ = ψ’(θ) is the Legendre transform

Exercise 3.5 (Applications). Implement the Information Geometric EM (IGEM) algorithm that uses α-projections for clustering multivariate Gaussian data. Compare convergence rates for different α values and interpret the results geometrically.

Exercise 3.6 (Advanced Computational). For a simple Bayesian linear regression model y = Xβ + ε with ε ~ N(0,σ²I), implement: - The joint statistical manifold for (β,σ²) - α-connection coefficients
- Visualization of the posterior manifold geometry - Geodesic paths for different α values

Exercise 3.7 (Theoretical). Prove the generalized Pythagorean theorem (Theorem 3.4) for α-divergences. Show that this implies the triangle inequality for α-divergences takes a modified form involving the dual parameter α*.

Exercise 3.8 (Research Extension). Investigate the α-geometry of transformer attention mechanisms. Model the attention weights as points on a statistical manifold and analyze how different α-connections correspond to different attention computations.

2.10 Connections

The α-connections and duality structure developed in this chapter form the foundation for understanding optimization algorithms in machine learning (Chapter 5), where natural gradient descent emerges as geodesic flow under appropriate α-connections. The divergence measures studied here generalize to more complex settings in Chapter 4, while the dually flat structure becomes essential for understanding the information geometry of neural networks in Chapter 6. The geometric EM algorithm provides a bridge to the thermodynamic perspective explored in Chapter 7, where α-connections correspond to different ensemble structures in statistical mechanics.

2.11 References

Amari, S. (1985). Differential-Geometrical Methods in Statistics. Springer-Verlag.

Amari, S., & Nagaoka, H. (2000). Methods of Information Geometry. American Mathematical Society.

Banerjee, A., Merugu, S., Dhillon, I. S., & Ghosh, J. (2005). Clustering with Bregman divergences. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 6, 1705-1749.

Barndorff-Nielsen, O. E. (1978). Information and Exponential Families in Statistical Theory. Wiley.

Brown, L. D. (1986). Fundamentals of Statistical Exponential Families. Institute of Mathematical Statistics.

Eguchi, S. (1992). Geometry of minimum contrast estimators. Hiroshima Mathematical Journal, 22(3), 631-647.

Nielsen, F., & Barbaresco, F. (Eds.). (2013). Geometric Science of Information. Springer.

Rockafellar, R. T. (1970). Convex Analysis. Princeton University Press.

3 Chapter 4: Divergences and Information Measures

The geometry of statistical manifolds naturally gives rise to a rich theory of divergences—asymmetric distance-like functions that measure the dissimilarity between probability distributions. While the Fisher information metric provides a Riemannian structure and defines geodesic distances, divergences offer more flexible and computationally tractable measures of statistical distance that arise naturally in inference, learning, and thermodynamics.

This chapter develops the theory of information divergences, beginning with the fundamental Kullback-Leibler divergence and its geometric interpretation. We explore the broader family of f-divergences, establish their relationships to Bregman divergences through the geometry of convex functions, and contrast information-theoretic measures with optimal transport distances. Throughout, we emphasize both the theoretical foundations and practical applications in statistical inference and machine learning.

3.1 The Kullback-Leibler Divergence

The Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence stands as perhaps the most fundamental information measure in statistics and information theory. Its geometric interpretation on statistical manifolds provides deep insights into the structure of statistical inference.

Definition 4.1 (Kullback-Leibler Divergence). Let P and Q be probability measures on a measurable space (Ω, ℱ) with P absolutely continuous with respect to Q. The Kullback-Leibler divergence from Q to P is \[D_{\text{KL}}(P \| Q) = \int \log\left(\frac{dP}{dQ}\right) dP = \mathbb{E}_P\left[\log\left(\frac{dP}{dQ}\right)\right]\] where dP/dQ is the Radon-Nikodym derivative. If P is not absolutely continuous with respect to Q, we define D_KL(P ∥ Q) = ∞.

For discrete distributions with probability mass functions p(x) and q(x), this reduces to the familiar form: \[D_{\text{KL}}(P \| Q) = \sum_x p(x) \log\left(\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right)\]

The KL divergence possesses several fundamental properties that distinguish it from true distance functions:

Theorem 4.1 (Properties of KL Divergence). The KL divergence satisfies: 1. Non-negativity: D_KL(P ∥ Q) ≥ 0 with equality if and only if P = Q almost surely 2. Asymmetry: D_KL(P ∥ Q) ≠ D_KL(Q ∥ P) in general 3. Convexity: D_KL(·∥Q) and D_KL(P∥·) are convex functionals 4. Additivity: For independent random variables, D_KL(P₁ × P₂ ∥ Q₁ × Q₂) = D_KL(P₁ ∥ Q₁) + D_KL(P₂ ∥ Q₂)

Proof. Non-negativity follows from Jensen’s inequality applied to the convex function -log: \[-D_{\text{KL}}(P \| Q) = \mathbb{E}_P\left[\log\left(\frac{dQ}{dP}\right)\right] \leq \log\left(\mathbb{E}_P\left[\frac{dQ}{dP}\right]\right) = \log(1) = 0\]

Equality holds if and only if dQ/dP is constant P-almost surely, which implies P = Q. The remaining properties follow from the convexity of the log function and the linearity of expectation. □

3.1.1 Connection to the Fisher Metric

The KL divergence has a profound geometric interpretation in terms of the Fisher information metric. Consider a parametric statistical model {p_θ : θ ∈ Θ} with θ ∈ ℝᵈ.

Theorem 4.2 (KL Divergence and Fisher Metric). For a regular parametric family, the second-order approximation of the KL divergence around θ is given by: \[D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\theta+\delta\theta} \| p_\theta) = \frac{1}{2} \delta\theta^T G(\theta) \delta\theta + O(|\delta\theta|^3)\] where G(θ) is the Fisher information matrix.

Proof. Using Taylor expansion of the log-likelihood: \[\log p_{\theta+\delta\theta}(x) = \log p_\theta(x) + \delta\theta^T \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(x) + \frac{1}{2}\delta\theta^T H_\theta(x) \delta\theta + O(|\delta\theta|^3)\]

Taking expectation under p_θ and using the fact that 𝔼[∇_θ log p_θ] = 0 (score function property): \[D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\theta+\delta\theta} \| p_\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\delta\theta^T \mathbb{E}[H_\theta] \delta\theta + O(|\delta\theta|^3)\]

Since 𝔼[H_θ] = -G(θ) by the information matrix equality, the result follows. □

This theorem reveals that the Fisher metric is the local Riemannian approximation to the KL divergence geometry. The geodesic distance on the statistical manifold can be interpreted as the “integrated” form of this local relationship.

3.1.2 Computational Implementation

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff, Plots

"""
    kl_divergence(P::Distribution, Q::Distribution, n_samples::Int=10000)

Estimate KL divergence D(P||Q) using Monte Carlo sampling.
"""
function kl_divergence(P::Distribution, Q::Distribution, n_samples::Int=10000)
    samples = rand(P, n_samples)
    log_ratio = logpdf.(P, samples) - logpdf.(Q, samples)
    return mean(log_ratio)
end

"""
    kl_divergence_discrete(p::Vector, q::Vector)

Exact KL divergence for discrete distributions.
"""
function kl_divergence_discrete(p::Vector, q::Vector)
    @assert length(p) == length(q)
    @assert all(p .>= 0) && all(q .>= 0)
    @assert abs(sum(p) - 1) < 1e-10 && abs(sum(q) - 1) < 1e-10
    
    kl = 0.0
    for i in 1:length(p)
        if p[i] > 0 && q[i] > 0
            kl += p[i] * log(p[i] / q[i])
        elseif p[i] > 0 && q[i] == 0
            return Inf
        end
    end
    return kl
end

# Example: KL divergence between normal distributions
P = Normal(0, 1)
Q = Normal(0.5, 1.2)

# Monte Carlo estimate
kl_mc = kl_divergence(P, Q, 100000)

# Analytical formula for multivariate normals
function kl_normal(μ₁, Σ₁, μ₂, Σ₂)
    d = length(μ₁)
    Σ₂_inv = inv(Σ₂)
    return 0.5 * (tr(Σ₂_inv * Σ₁) + (μ₂ - μ₁)' * Σ₂_inv * (μ₂ - μ₁) - d + logdet(Σ₂) - logdet(Σ₁))
end

# For univariate case
μ₁, σ₁² = 0.0, 1.0
μ₂, σ₂² = 0.5, 1.44
kl_exact = 0.5 * (σ₁²/σ₂² + (μ₂ - μ₁)²/σ₂² - 1 + log(σ₂²/σ₁²))

println("Monte Carlo estimate: $(round(kl_mc, digits=4))")
println("Exact value: $(round(kl_exact, digits=4))")

3.2 f-Divergences

The KL divergence belongs to a broader class of information measures known as f-divergences, which provide a unified framework for understanding various notions of statistical distance.

Definition 4.2 (f-Divergence). Let f: (0,∞) → ℝ be a convex function with f(1) = 0. For probability measures P and Q with P absolutely continuous with respect to Q, the f-divergence is defined as: \[D_f(P \| Q) = \int f\left(\frac{dP}{dQ}\right) dQ\]

The convexity of f ensures that f-divergences inherit many desirable properties. Different choices of f lead to well-known divergences:

3.2.1 Hellinger Distance

Setting f(t) = (√t - 1)²/2 yields the squared Hellinger distance: \[H^2(P, Q) = \frac{1}{2}\int \left(\sqrt{p} - \sqrt{q}\right)^2 d\mu\]

Unlike KL divergence, the Hellinger distance is symmetric and satisfies the triangle inequality, making it a true metric on probability distributions.

3.2.2 Chi-Squared Divergence

The function f(t) = (t-1)² gives the chi-squared divergence: \[\chi^2(P \| Q) = \int \frac{(p-q)^2}{q} d\mu\]

This divergence appears naturally in goodness-of-fit testing and has strong connections to statistical hypothesis testing.

3.2.3 Total Variation Distance

The choice f(t) = ½|t-1| leads to the total variation distance: \[\text{TV}(P, Q) = \frac{1}{2}\int |p - q| d\mu = \sup_{A \in \mathcal{F}} |P(A) - Q(A)|\]

Total variation has the interpretation as the maximum difference in probability that P and Q assign to any measurable event.

Theorem 4.3 (Properties of f-Divergences). All f-divergences satisfy: 1. Non-negativity: D_f(P ∥ Q) ≥ f(1) = 0 2. Joint convexity: D_f(λP₁ + (1-λ)P₂ ∥ λQ₁ + (1-λ)Q₂) ≤ λD_f(P₁ ∥ Q₁) + (1-λ)D_f(P₂ ∥ Q₂) 3. Monotonicity: D_f(T(P) ∥ T(Q)) ≤ D_f(P ∥ Q) for any Markov kernel T

Proof. Properties follow from Jensen’s inequality and the convexity of f. The monotonicity property reflects the data processing inequality—statistical distance cannot increase under randomization. □

3.2.4 Implementation of f-Divergences

"""
    f_divergence(f, P::Distribution, Q::Distribution, n_samples::Int=10000)

Estimate f-divergence using Monte Carlo integration.
"""
function f_divergence(f, P::Distribution, Q::Distribution, n_samples::Int=10000)
    # Sample from Q for integration
    samples = rand(Q, n_samples)
    density_ratios = exp.(logpdf.(P, samples) - logpdf.(Q, samples))
    return mean(f.(density_ratios))
end

# Define specific f-divergences
hellinger_f(t) = 0.5 * (sqrt(t) - 1)^2
chi_squared_f(t) = (t - 1)^2
total_variation_f(t) = 0.5 * abs(t - 1)

# Example: compare different f-divergences
P = Normal(0, 1)
Q = Normal(0.3, 1.1)

kl_div = kl_divergence(P, Q)
hellinger_div = f_divergence(hellinger_f, P, Q)
chi2_div = f_divergence(chi_squared_f, P, Q)
tv_div = f_divergence(total_variation_f, P, Q)

println("KL divergence: $(round(kl_div, digits=4))")
println("Hellinger distance²: $(round(hellinger_div, digits=4))")
println("Chi-squared divergence: $(round(chi2_div, digits=4))")
println("Total variation: $(round(tv_div, digits=4))")

# Analytical Hellinger distance for normal distributions
function hellinger_normal(μ₁, σ₁, μ₂, σ₂)
    σ₁₂ = sqrt(2σ₁*σ₂/(σ₁² + σ₂²))
    return 1 - σ₁₂/sqrt(σ₁*σ₂) * exp(-0.25 * (μ₁-μ₂)²/(σ₁²+σ₂²))
end

hellinger_exact = hellinger_normal(0.0, 1.0, 0.3, 1.1)
println("Exact Hellinger²: $(round(hellinger_exact, digits=4))")

3.3 Rényi Divergences

Rényi divergences provide a parametric family of information measures that interpolate between different limiting cases and have found applications in privacy, learning theory, and thermodynamics.

Definition 4.3 (Rényi Divergence). For α > 0, α ≠ 1, and probability measures P, Q with P absolutely continuous with respect to Q, the Rényi divergence of order α is: \[D_\alpha(P \| Q) = \frac{1}{\alpha-1} \log \int \left(\frac{dP}{dQ}\right)^\alpha dQ\]

The Rényi divergence family exhibits several important limiting behaviors: - As α → 1: D₁(P ∥ Q) = D_KL(P ∥ Q) (Kullback-Leibler) - α = 0: D₀(P ∥ Q) = -log(support(Q) ∩ support(P)) - α = 2: D₂(P ∥ Q) = log ∫(p²/q)dμ (related to chi-squared) - α → ∞: D_∞(P ∥ Q) = log(ess sup(p/q))

Theorem 4.4 (Monotonicity of Rényi Divergences). For 0 < α < β, we have: \[D_\alpha(P \| Q) \geq D_\beta(P \| Q)\]

Proof. This follows from Hölder’s inequality applied to the integral defining the Rényi divergence. The proof uses the fact that the function α ↦ D_α(P ∥ Q) is decreasing. □

3.3.1 Connection to Statistical Physics

Rényi divergences arise naturally in statistical mechanics and thermodynamics. The Rényi entropy S_α(P) = (1-α)⁻¹ log ∫ p^α dx generalizes the Shannon entropy (α = 1) and connects to the partition function in statistical physics.

using SpecialFunctions

"""
    renyi_divergence(α, P::Distribution, Q::Distribution, n_samples::Int=10000)

Estimate Rényi divergence of order α using Monte Carlo sampling.
"""
function renyi_divergence(α, P::Distribution, Q::Distribution, n_samples::Int=10000)
    if abs(α - 1.0) < 1e-10
        return kl_divergence(P, Q, n_samples)
    end
    
    samples = rand(Q, n_samples)
    density_ratios = exp.(logpdf.(P, samples) - logpdf.(Q, samples))
    integral_estimate = mean(density_ratios .^ α)
    
    return log(integral_estimate) / (α - 1)
end

"""
    renyi_divergence_gaussian(α, μ₁, σ₁, μ₂, σ₂)

Analytical Rényi divergence between two univariate Gaussian distributions.
"""
function renyi_divergence_gaussian(α, μ₁, σ₁, μ₂, σ₂)
    if abs(α - 1.0) < 1e-10
        # KL divergence case
        return 0.5 * (σ₁²/σ₂² + (μ₂ - μ₁)²/σ₂² - 1 + log(σ₂²/σ₁²))
    end
    
    σ_α² = α * σ₁² + (1 - α) * σ₂²
    
    if σ_α² <= 0
        return Inf
    end
    
    log_term = 0.5 * log(σ_α² / (σ₁^(2α) * σ₂^(2(1-α))))
    exp_term = α * (1 - α) * (μ₁ - μ₂)² / (2 * σ_α²)
    
    return (log_term + exp_term) / (α - 1)
end

# Example: Rényi divergence profile
α_values = [0.1, 0.5, 0.9, 1.1, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0]
P = Normal(0, 1)
Q = Normal(0.5, 1.2)

renyi_values = []
for α in α_values
    if abs(α - 1.0) < 1e-10
        push!(renyi_values, kl_divergence(P, Q))
    else
        push!(renyi_values, renyi_divergence_gaussian(α, 0.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.2))
    end
end

# Plot Rényi divergence profile
# [FIGURE: Plot of Rényi divergence vs α showing monotonic decrease]
println("Rényi divergence profile:")
for (α, d_α) in zip(α_values, renyi_values)
    println("α = $(α): D_α = $(round(d_α, digits=4))")
end

3.4 Bregman Divergences

Bregman divergences emerge from the geometry of convex functions and provide a natural bridge between information geometry and convex optimization. They are characterized by a unique geometric property—the Pythagorean relationship—which has profound implications for clustering, projection, and optimization algorithms.

Definition 4.4 (Bregman Divergence). Let φ: S → ℝ be a strictly convex, differentiable function on a convex set S ⊆ ℝᵈ. The Bregman divergence generated by φ is: \[D_\phi(x, y) = \phi(x) - \phi(y) - \nabla\phi(y)^T(x - y)\]

Geometrically, D_φ(x,y) measures the difference between φ(x) and the first-order Taylor approximation of φ at y evaluated at x.

3.4.1 Examples of Bregman Divergences

  1. Squared Euclidean Distance: φ(x) = ½∥x∥² gives D_φ(x,y) = ½∥x-y∥²
  2. KL Divergence: On the probability simplex with φ(p) = Σᵢ pᵢ log pᵢ gives D_φ(p,q) = D_KL(p∥q)
  3. Exponential Loss: φ(x) = e^x yields D_φ(x,y) = e^x - e^y - ye^y(x-y)
  4. Logistic Loss: φ(x) = log(1 + e^x) for binary classification

Theorem 4.5 (Pythagorean Property). Bregman divergences satisfy the generalized Pythagorean theorem: for any convex set C and x ∉ C, if y* is the Bregman projection of x onto C, then for all y ∈ C: \[D_\phi(x, y) = D_\phi(x, y^*) + D_\phi(y^*, y)\]

Proof. The Bregman projection y* satisfies the optimality condition (∇φ(y) - ∇φ(x))ᵀ(y - y) ≥ 0 for all y ∈ C. The Pythagorean identity follows by expanding the definitions and using this variational characterization. □

This property is fundamental in clustering algorithms like Bregman k-means and in understanding the geometry of exponential families.

3.4.2 Uniqueness of Bregman Divergences

Theorem 4.6 (Characterization of Bregman Divergences). A divergence D(x,y) on a convex domain arises from a Bregman generator if and only if it satisfies: 1. Convexity: D(·,y) is convex for all y 2. Pythagorean Property: D(x,z) = D(x,y) + D(y,z) when z = arg min_{w∈C} D(x,w) and y ∈ C

This characterization shows that the Pythagorean property essentially defines Bregman divergences among all convex divergences.

"""
    bregman_divergence(φ, ∇φ, x, y)

Compute Bregman divergence D_φ(x,y) = φ(x) - φ(y) - ∇φ(y)ᵀ(x-y).
"""
function bregman_divergence(φ, ∇φ, x, y)
    return φ(x) - φ(y) - dot(∇φ(y), x - y)
end

# Example 1: Squared Euclidean distance
φ_squared(x) = 0.5 * dot(x, x)
∇φ_squared(x) = x

x1, y1 = [1.0, 2.0], [3.0, 1.0]
d_euclidean = bregman_divergence(φ_squared, ∇φ_squared, x1, y1)
println("Squared Euclidean distance: $(d_euclidean)")
println("Direct computation: $(0.5 * norm(x1 - y1)^2)")

# Example 2: KL divergence on probability simplex
function φ_entropy(p)
    return sum(pi > 0 ? pi * log(pi) : 0.0 for pi in p)
end

function ∇φ_entropy(p)
    return [pi > 0 ? log(pi) + 1 : -Inf for pi in p]
end

# Probability vectors
p = [0.6, 0.3, 0.1]
q = [0.4, 0.4, 0.2]

d_kl_bregman = bregman_divergence(φ_entropy, ∇φ_entropy, p, q)
d_kl_direct = kl_divergence_discrete(p, q)

println("KL divergence via Bregman: $(round(d_kl_bregman, digits=6))")
