println("KL divergence direct: $(round(d_kl_direct, digits=6))")

# Example 3: Exponential Bregman divergence
φ_exp(x) = exp(x[1])  # Univariate case
∇φ_exp(x) = [exp(x[1])]

x_exp, y_exp = [2.0], [1.0]
d_exp = bregman_divergence(φ_exp, ∇φ_exp, x_exp, y_exp)
println("Exponential Bregman divergence: $(d_exp)")

3.5 Wasserstein Distance vs Information Divergences

The Wasserstein distance, also known as the Earth Mover’s Distance, represents a fundamentally different approach to measuring dissimilarity between probability distributions. Unlike information-theoretic divergences that compare densities pointwise, Wasserstein distances incorporate the underlying metric structure of the sample space.

Definition 4.5 (Wasserstein Distance). Let (X,d) be a metric space and let Π(P,Q) denote the set of all couplings (joint distributions) with marginals P and Q. The p-Wasserstein distance is: \[W_p(P, Q) = \left(\inf_{\gamma \in \Π(P,Q)} \mathbb{E}_{(X,Y) \sim \gamma}[d(X,Y)^p]\right)^{1/p}\]

For p = 1, this is the Kantorovich distance, and for p = 2, the 2-Wasserstein distance has particularly nice properties related to optimal transport.

3.5.1 Fundamental Differences

The contrast between Wasserstein distances and information divergences reveals different geometric structures:

  1. Support Sensitivity: Information divergences are highly sensitive to support differences—if P has support where Q doesn’t, KL divergence becomes infinite. Wasserstein distance remains finite if the supports are at finite distance.

  2. Metricity: Wasserstein distances are true metrics (symmetric, satisfy triangle inequality), while most information divergences are not.

  3. Weak Topology: Wasserstein distance metrizes weak convergence of probability measures, making it natural for studying convergence in distribution.

  4. Computational Tractability: KL and other f-divergences often have closed forms for parametric families, while Wasserstein distances typically require numerical optimization.

Example 4.1 (Wasserstein vs KL for Shifted Gaussians). Consider P = N(0,1) and Q = N(μ,1) with μ → ∞. The KL divergence grows as D_KL(P∥Q) = μ²/2, while the 2-Wasserstein distance is simply W₂(P,Q) = |μ|. This shows how Wasserstein distance provides a more stable notion of distance for distributions with non-overlapping support.

using OptimalTransport, Distances

"""
    wasserstein_1d(x, p, y, q)

Compute 1-Wasserstein distance between discrete distributions on R.
x, y are support points, p, q are probability masses.
"""
function wasserstein_1d(x, p, y, q)
    # For 1D, Wasserstein distance has closed form via quantiles
    n, m = length(x), length(y)
    
    # Sort supports with their probabilities
    perm_x = sortperm(x)
    perm_y = sortperm(y)
    
    x_sorted, p_sorted = x[perm_x], p[perm_x]
    y_sorted, q_sorted = y[perm_y], q[perm_y]
    
    # Compute cumulative distributions
    F_x = cumsum([0; p_sorted])
    F_y = cumsum([0; q_sorted])
    
    # Integrate |F_x - F_y|
    all_points = sort(unique([x_sorted; y_sorted]))
    distance = 0.0
    
    for i in 1:(length(all_points)-1)
        x_left, x_right = all_points[i], all_points[i+1]
        
        # Find F_x and F_y at this interval
        fx = F_x[findlast(x_sorted .<= x_left)]
        fy = F_y[findlast(y_sorted .<= x_left)]
        
        distance += abs(fx - fy) * (x_right - x_left)
    end
    
    return distance
end

# Example: Compare Wasserstein and KL for shifted distributions
shifts = 0:0.5:5
kl_values = Float64[]
w_values = Float64[]

for μ in shifts
    # Discrete approximations of N(0,1) and N(μ,1)
    x_range = -5:0.1:5
    p1 = pdf.(Normal(0, 1), x_range)
    p1 = p1 / sum(p1)  # Normalize
    
    p2 = pdf.(Normal(μ, 1), x_range)  
    p2 = p2 / sum(p2)  # Normalize
    
    # KL divergence
    kl = kl_divergence_discrete(p1, p2)
    push!(kl_values, kl)
    
    # Wasserstein distance
    w = wasserstein_1d(collect(x_range), p1, collect(x_range), p2)
    push!(w_values, w)
end

println("Shift\tKL Div\t\tWasserstein")
for (i, μ) in enumerate(shifts)
    println("$(μ)\t$(round(kl_values[i], digits=3))\t\t$(round(w_values[i], digits=3))")
end

# Theoretical values for comparison
theoretical_kl = 0.5 * shifts.^2
theoretical_w2 = abs.(shifts)  # For 2-Wasserstein, but 1-Wasserstein is similar

println("\nTheoretical KL (μ²/2): ", round.(theoretical_kl, digits=3))
println("Theoretical |μ|: ", round.(theoretical_w2, digits=3))

3.6 Fisher-Rao Distance

The Fisher-Rao distance represents the natural geodesic distance on the statistical manifold equipped with the Fisher information metric. Unlike the asymmetric information divergences, it provides a true Riemannian distance that respects the geometric structure of probability distributions.

Definition 4.6 (Fisher-Rao Distance). For a parametric statistical model {p_θ : θ ∈ Θ}, the Fisher-Rao distance between distributions p_θ₁ and p_θ₂ is the geodesic distance on the statistical manifold: \[d_{\text{FR}}(p_{\theta_1}, p_{\theta_2}) = \inf_{\gamma} \int_0^1 \sqrt{\gamma'(t)^T G(\gamma(t)) \gamma'(t)} \, dt\] where the infimum is taken over all smooth curves γ(t) with γ(0) = θ₁ and γ(1) = θ₂, and G(θ) is the Fisher information matrix.

For many important families, the Fisher-Rao distance has closed-form expressions:

Example 4.2 (Fisher-Rao Distance for Gaussian Distributions). For the family of univariate Gaussians N(μ,σ²), the Fisher-Rao distance is: \[d_{\text{FR}}(N(\mu_1,\sigma_1^2), N(\mu_2,\sigma_2^2)) = \sqrt{(\mu_1-\mu_2)^2/\sigma_1^2 + 2\log^2(\sigma_2/\sigma_1)}\]

The Fisher-Rao geometry has deep connections to other geometric structures:

  1. Sphere Representation: The Fisher-Rao metric on discrete probability distributions (the probability simplex) is equivalent to the round metric on the unit sphere via the square-root map p ↦ √p.

  2. Information Geodesics: Geodesics in the Fisher-Rao geometry correspond to exponential families with linear sufficient statistics.

  3. Optimal Transport Connection: Recent work has established connections between Fisher-Rao distance and optimal transport with quadratic cost on the space of measures.

3.6.1 Computational Aspects

Computing Fisher-Rao distances typically requires solving geodesic equations, which can be computationally intensive. However

4 Chapter 5: Natural Gradient Descent

The optimization landscape of statistical models exhibits a rich geometric structure that standard gradient descent methods fail to exploit. When we parameterize a statistical model, the choice of coordinates affects the behavior of gradient descent, leading to inefficient optimization paths that depend arbitrarily on our parameterization. Natural gradient descent resolves this issue by performing steepest descent with respect to the Fisher information metric, yielding optimization algorithms that are invariant to smooth reparameterizations and often converge more rapidly than their Euclidean counterparts.

This chapter develops the theory and practice of natural gradient descent from first principles. We establish the connection between the Fisher information matrix and Riemannian optimization, derive the natural gradient update rule, and explore its intimate relationship with Newton’s method. For exponential families, we show that natural gradients admit particularly elegant closed-form expressions. We then examine modern applications including neural network optimization through K-FAC approximations, policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning, and variational inference algorithms.

4.1 Geometric Foundations of Natural Gradient

4.1.1 The Parameterization Problem

Consider a parametric family of probability distributions {p(x|θ) : θ ∈ Θ ⊆ ℝᵈ} where θ represents the parameters. When optimizing an objective function L(θ), standard gradient descent performs the update:

θ^(t+1) = θ^(t) - η ∇L(θ^(t))

This update rule depends critically on our choice of parameterization. If we perform a smooth reparameterization θ = φ(ξ), the gradient transforms as:

∇_ξ L = (∂φ/∂ξ)ᵀ ∇_θ L

The Euclidean gradient descent in the ξ-parameterization becomes:

ξ^(t+1) = ξ^(t) - η (∂φ/∂ξ)ᵀ ∇_θ L

When we transform back to θ-coordinates, this yields a different update than standard gradient descent in θ. This parameterization dependence is problematic: the optimization path should reflect the intrinsic geometry of the statistical model, not our arbitrary choice of coordinates.

Definition 5.1 (Riemannian Gradient). Let (M, g) be a Riemannian manifold and f: M → ℝ a smooth function. The Riemannian gradient ∇^g f at point p ∈ M is the unique tangent vector satisfying:

g_p(∇^g f, v) = df_p(v)

for all tangent vectors v ∈ T_p M.

For a parameterized manifold with coordinates θ and metric tensor g_ij, the Riemannian gradient has components:

(∇^g f)^i = g^{ij} ∂f/∂θʲ

where g^{ij} are the components of the inverse metric tensor.

4.1.2 The Fisher Information Metric

The natural Riemannian metric on a statistical manifold is the Fisher information metric, defined in Chapter 1. Recall that for a parametric family p(x|θ), the Fisher information matrix has components:

G_ij(θ) = E_p[∂/∂θⁱ log p(x|θ) · ∂/∂θʲ log p(x|θ)]

This metric tensor encodes the local geometry of the statistical manifold. The natural gradient with respect to the Fisher metric is:

∇̃L = G⁻¹(θ) ∇L(θ)

where ∇̃ denotes the natural gradient and ∇ the standard Euclidean gradient.

Theorem 5.1 (Parameterization Invariance of Natural Gradient). Let L(θ) be an objective function on a statistical manifold, and let θ = φ(ξ) be a smooth reparameterization. Then the natural gradient is invariant under this reparameterization in the sense that the natural gradient flow follows the same geometric path regardless of parameterization.

Proof. Under the reparameterization θ = φ(ξ), the Fisher information matrix transforms as:

G_ξ = (∂φ/∂ξ)ᵀ G_θ (∂φ/∂ξ)

The gradient of L transforms as ∇_ξ L = (∂φ/∂ξ)ᵀ ∇_θ L. The natural gradient in ξ-coordinates is:

∇̃_ξ L = G_ξ⁻¹ ∇_ξ L = [(∂φ/∂ξ)ᵀ G_θ (∂φ/∂ξ)]⁻¹ (∂φ/∂ξ)ᵀ ∇_θ L

Using the matrix identity (AᵀGA)⁻¹Aᵀ = A⁻¹G⁻¹, we obtain:

∇̃_ξ L = (∂φ/∂ξ)⁻¹ G_θ⁻¹ ∇_θ L

This shows that the natural gradient update in ξ-coordinates, when transformed back to θ-coordinates, yields exactly the natural gradient update in θ-coordinates. □

4.1.3 Natural Gradient Descent Algorithm

The natural gradient descent algorithm performs steepest descent with respect to the Fisher information metric:

θ^(t+1) = θ^(t) - η G⁻¹(θ^(t)) ∇L(θ^(t))

The Fisher information matrix G(θ) acts as a preconditioner that adapts the step direction based on the local geometry of the statistical manifold.

using LinearAlgebra, Distributions, ForwardDiff

"""
    natural_gradient_step(θ, grad_L, fisher_info, η)

Perform one step of natural gradient descent.

Arguments:
- θ: current parameters
- grad_L: gradient of loss function
- fisher_info: Fisher information matrix
- η: learning rate

Returns updated parameters.
"""
function natural_gradient_step(θ, grad_L, fisher_info, η)
    # Compute natural gradient by solving G * nat_grad = grad_L
    natural_grad = fisher_info \ grad_L
    return θ - η * natural_grad
end

"""
    fisher_information_gaussian(μ, σ²)

Compute Fisher information matrix for Gaussian distribution N(μ, σ²).
"""
function fisher_information_gaussian(μ, σ²)
    G = zeros(2, 2)
    G[1, 1] = 1 / σ²        # ∂²(-log L)/∂μ²
    G[2, 2] = 1 / (2 * σ²^2) # ∂²(-log L)/∂(σ²)²
    # Off-diagonal terms are zero
    return G
end

# Example: Maximum likelihood estimation for Gaussian distribution
function gaussian_mle_natural_gradient(data, θ_init, η, max_iter)
    θ = copy(θ_init)  # θ = [μ, σ²]
    n = length(data)
    
    for i in 1:max_iter
        μ, σ² = θ[1], θ[2]
        
        # Negative log-likelihood gradient
        grad_L = zeros(2)
        grad_L[1] = -(sum(data) - n * μ) / σ²  # ∂(-log L)/∂μ
        grad_L[2] = n/(2*σ²) - sum((data .- μ).^2)/(2*σ²^2)  # ∂(-log L)/∂σ²
        
        # Fisher information matrix
        G = fisher_information_gaussian(μ, σ²)
        
        # Natural gradient step
        θ = natural_gradient_step(θ, grad_L, G, η)
        
        # Ensure σ² > 0
        θ[2] = max(θ[2], 1e-6)
        
        if norm(grad_L) < 1e-8
            break
        end
    end
    
    return θ
end

# Generate synthetic data and test
Random.seed!(123)
true_μ, true_σ² = 2.5, 1.5
data = randn(100) * sqrt(true_σ²) .+ true_μ

# Compare with standard gradient descent
θ_init = [0.0, 1.0]
θ_natural = gaussian_mle_natural_gradient(data, θ_init, 0.1, 100)
println("Natural gradient result: μ̂ = $(θ_natural[1]), σ̂² = $(θ_natural[2])")
println("True parameters: μ = $true_μ, σ² = $true_σ²")

4.2 Connection to Newton’s Method

The natural gradient exhibits a profound connection to Newton’s method through the relationship between the Fisher information and the Hessian of the log-likelihood function.

4.2.1 Second-Order Properties

For maximum likelihood estimation with log-likelihood ℓ(θ) = Σᵢ log p(xᵢ|θ), the Hessian of the negative log-likelihood is:

H_ij = -∂²ℓ/∂θⁱ∂θʲ = -Σᵢ ∂²log p(xᵢ|θ)/∂θⁱ∂θʲ

By the Fisher information identity:

E[∂²log p(x|θ)/∂θⁱ∂θʲ] = -E[∂log p(x|θ)/∂θⁱ · ∂log p(x|θ)/∂θʲ] = -G_ij(θ)

Therefore, E[H] = nG(θ) where n is the sample size.

Theorem 5.2 (Natural Gradient as Approximate Newton). For maximum likelihood estimation, the natural gradient method approximates Newton’s method by replacing the empirical Hessian with its expectation:

θ^(t+1) ≈ θ^(t) - [E[H]]⁻¹ ∇ℓ(θ^(t)) = θ^(t) - (nG(θ^(t)))⁻¹ ∇ℓ(θ^(t))

This connection explains why natural gradient often exhibits superlinear convergence properties similar to Newton’s method, while avoiding the computational cost and potential instability of computing second derivatives.

4.2.2 Gauss-Newton Connection

For generalized linear models and other exponential family problems, the natural gradient coincides exactly with the Gauss-Newton approximation to Newton’s method. This provides theoretical justification for the superior convergence properties observed in practice.

4.3 Natural Gradient for Exponential Families

Exponential families, introduced in Chapter 2, admit particularly elegant natural gradient formulations due to their canonical geometric structure.

4.3.1 Canonical Form and Natural Parameters

Recall that an exponential family in canonical form has density:

p(x|η) = h(x) exp(ηᵀT(x) - A(η))

where η are the natural parameters, T(x) are the sufficient statistics, and A(η) is the log-partition function.

Theorem 5.3 (Natural Gradient for Exponential Families). For an exponential family in canonical form, the Fisher information matrix is the Hessian of the log-partition function:

G_ij(η) = ∂²A(η)/∂ηⁱ∂ηʲ

The natural gradient for any objective function L(η) is:

∇̃L(η) = [∇²A(η)]⁻¹ ∇L(η)

Proof. The score function is:

∂log p(x|η)/∂ηⁱ = Tⁱ(x) - ∂A(η)/∂ηⁱ

The Fisher information is:

G_ij = E[(Tⁱ(x) - ∂A/∂ηⁱ)(Tʲ(x) - ∂A/∂ηʲ)] = E[Tⁱ(x)Tʲ(x)] - ∂A/∂ηⁱ ∂A/∂ηʲ

Since E[Tⁱ(x)] = ∂A/∂ηⁱ and Cov(Tⁱ(x), Tʲ(x)) = E[Tⁱ(x)Tʲ(x)] - E[Tⁱ(x)]E[Tʲ(x)], we obtain:

G_ij = Cov(Tⁱ(x), Tʲ(x)) = ∂²A(η)/∂ηⁱ∂ηʲ

The last equality follows from the cumulant generating function property of A(η). □

4.3.2 Worked Example: Natural Gradient for Gaussian Distribution

Consider the multivariate Gaussian distribution N(μ, Σ) parameterized in natural coordinates. The natural parameters are:

η₁ = Σ⁻¹μ η₂ = -½Σ⁻¹

The log-partition function is:

A(η₁, η₂) = -¼η₁ᵀη₂⁻¹η₁ - ½log|−2η₂|

using LinearAlgebra

"""
    gaussian_natural_gradient(η₁, η₂, grad_L)

Compute natural gradient for multivariate Gaussian in natural parameterization.

Arguments:
- η₁: first natural parameter Σ⁻¹μ
- η₂: second natural parameter -½Σ⁻¹
- grad_L: gradient of objective function

Returns natural gradient.
"""
function gaussian_natural_gradient(η₁, η₂, grad_L)
    d = length(η₁)
    
    # Compute mean and covariance from natural parameters
    Σ = -0.5 * inv(η₂)
    μ = Σ * η₁
    
    # Fisher information matrix (Hessian of log-partition function)
    G = zeros(d + d*d, d + d*d)
    
    # Block structure of Fisher information
    G[1:d, 1:d] = Σ  # G₁₁
    
    # G₂₂ block (more complex for matrix parameter)
    for i in 1:d, j in 1:d
        for k in 1:d, l in 1:d
            idx1 = d + (i-1)*d + j
            idx2 = d + (k-1)*d + l
            G[idx1, idx2] = 0.5 * (Σ[i,k]*Σ[j,l] + Σ[i,l]*Σ[j,k])
        end
    end
    
    # G₁₂ and G₂₁ blocks
    for i in 1:d
        for j in 1:d, k in 1:d
            idx = d + (j-1)*d + k
            G[i, idx] = G[idx, i] = (i == j ? μ[k] : 0.0)
        end
    end
    
    return G \ grad_L
end

"""
    multivariate_gaussian_mle_natural(data, max_iter=100, η=0.01)

Maximum likelihood estimation for multivariate Gaussian using natural gradient.
"""
function multivariate_gaussian_mle_natural(data, max_iter=100, η=0.01)
    n, d = size(data)
    
    # Initialize natural parameters
    μ_init = mean(data, dims=1)[1,:]
    Σ_init = cov(data')
    
    η₁ = Σ_init \ μ_init
    η₂ = -0.5 * inv(Σ_init)
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # Current mean and covariance
        Σ = -0.5 * inv(η₂)
        μ = Σ * η₁
        
        # Negative log-likelihood gradient
        grad_L = zeros(d + d*d)
        
        # Gradient w.r.t. η₁
        grad_L[1:d] = -sum(data .- μ', dims=1)' / n
        
        # Gradient w.r.t. η₂ (vectorized)
        S = cov(data') * (n-1) / n + (mean(data, dims=1)[1,:] - μ) * (mean(data, dims=1)[1,:] - μ)'
        grad_η₂ = 0.5 * (inv(Σ) - S * inv(Σ) * inv(Σ))
        grad_L[d+1:end] = vec(grad_η₂)
        
        # Natural gradient step
        nat_grad = gaussian_natural_gradient(η₁, η₂, grad_L)
        
        # Update parameters
        η₁ -= η * nat_grad[1:d]
        η₂_vec = vec(η₂) - η * nat_grad[d+1:end]
        η₂ = reshape(η₂_vec, d, d)
        
        # Check convergence
        if norm(grad_L) < 1e-8
            println("Converged at iteration $iter")
            break
        end
    end
    
    # Convert back to mean-covariance parameterization
    Σ_final = -0.5 * inv(η₂)
    μ_final = Σ_final * η₁
    
    return μ_final, Σ_final
end

4.4 Applications in Machine Learning

Natural gradient descent has found widespread application in modern machine learning, where the geometric structure of parameter spaces often leads to poorly conditioned optimization landscapes.

4.4.1 K-FAC: Efficient Natural Gradient for Neural Networks

Computing the full Fisher information matrix for neural networks is prohibitively expensive, scaling as O(p²) where p is the number of parameters. The Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature (K-FAC) method provides a practical approximation by exploiting the layered structure of neural networks.

For a layer with weight matrix W and input activations A, the Kronecker factorization approximates the Fisher information block as:

G_W ≈ E[AA^T] ⊗ E[∇_W L (∇_W L)^T]

This reduces storage from O(p²) to O(p) and computation from O(p³) to O(p^(3/2)).

using Flux, LinearAlgebra

"""
    KFACOptimizer

K-FAC optimizer implementing Kronecker-factored natural gradient approximation.
"""
mutable struct KFACOptimizer
    η::Float64                    # Learning rate
    λ::Float64                    # Damping parameter
    update_freq::Int              # Frequency of covariance matrix updates
    step_count::Int
    A_factors::Dict               # Input covariance factors
    S_factors::Dict               # Gradient covariance factors
end

function KFACOptimizer(η=0.001, λ=1e-4, update_freq=10)
    return KFACOptimizer(η, λ, update_freq, 0, Dict(), Dict())
end

"""
    update_factors!(opt, layer_id, A, S)

Update Kronecker factors for a given layer.
A: input activations (including bias term)
S: output gradients
"""
function update_factors!(opt::KFACOptimizer, layer_id, A, S)
    # Exponential moving average for covariance factors
    α = 0.95  # Decay rate
    
    # Input covariance factor
    AA = A * A'
    if haskey(opt.A_factors, layer_id)
        opt.A_factors[layer_id] = α * opt.A_factors[layer_id] + (1-α) * AA
    else
        opt.A_factors[layer_id] = AA
    end
    
    # Output gradient covariance factor  
    SS = S * S'
    if haskey(opt.S_factors, layer_id)
        opt.S_factors[layer_id] = α * opt.S_factors[layer_id] + (1-α) * SS
    else
        opt.S_factors[layer_id] = SS
    end
end

"""
    kfac_update!(W, grad_W, A_inv, S_inv, η)

Apply K-FAC natural gradient update to weight matrix.
"""
function kfac_update!(W, grad_W, A_inv, S_inv, η)
    # Natural gradient approximation: (S ⊗ A)^(-1) vec(grad_W) = vec(S_inv * grad_W * A_inv)
    nat_grad_W = S_inv * grad_W * A_inv
    W .-= η * nat_grad_W
    return W
end

"""
    apply_kfac_step!(model, opt)

Apply one K-FAC optimization step to the model.
"""
function apply_kfac_step!(model, opt::KFACOptimizer)
    opt.step_count += 1
    
    # Update factors periodically
    if opt.step_count % opt.update_freq == 0
        # In practice, this would be called during forward/backward pass
        # to collect activations and gradients
    end
    
    # Apply natural gradient updates
    for (layer_id, layer) in enumerate(model.layers)
        if haskey(opt.A_factors, layer_id) && haskey(opt.S_factors, layer_id)
            A_factor = opt.A_factors[layer_id] + opt.λ * I
            S_factor = opt.S_factors[layer_id] + opt.λ * I
            
            A_inv = inv(A_factor)
            S_inv = inv(S_factor)
            
            # Update weights using natural gradient
            if haskey(layer, :weight) && layer.weight isa AbstractMatrix
                grad_W = Flux.gradient(() -> loss(model, batch), Flux.params(layer.weight))[layer.weight]
                kfac_update!(layer.weight, grad_W, A_inv, S_inv, opt.η)
            end
        end
    end
end

# Example usage for a simple feedforward network
function train_with_kfac(model, train_data, epochs=10)
    opt = KFACOptimizer(0.01, 1e-3, 5)
    
    for epoch in 1:epochs
        for batch in train_data
            # Forward pass (collect activations here in practice)
            loss_val = loss(model, batch)
            
            # Backward pass (collect gradients here in practice)  
            grads = Flux.gradient(() -> loss(model, batch), Flux.params(model))
            
            # Apply K-FAC update
            apply_kfac_step!(model, opt)
            
            println("Epoch $epoch, Loss: $loss_val")
        end
    end
    
    return model
end

4.4.2 Natural Policy Gradients in Reinforcement Learning

In reinforcement learning, policy gradient methods optimize a parameterized policy π(a|s; θ) to maximize expected cumulative reward. The policy gradient theorem gives:

∇J(θ) = E[∇ log π(a|s; θ) Q(s,a)]

where Q(s,a) is the action-value function. The natural policy gradient uses the Fisher information metric of the policy:

G_ij(θ) = E[∂ log π(a|s; θ)/∂θⁱ · ∂ log π(a|s; θ)/∂θʲ]

The natural gradient becomes:

∇̃J(θ) = G⁻¹(θ) ∇J(θ)

This leads to faster convergence and better sample efficiency compared to standard policy gradients.

using Distributions, Random

"""
    NaturalPolicyGradient

Natural policy gradient optimizer for reinforcement learning.
"""
struct NaturalPolicyGradient
    η::Float64              # Learning rate
    λ::Float64              # Regularization parameter
    batch_size::Int         # Mini-batch size
end

"""
    gaussian_policy_fisher_information(θ, states, σ²)

Compute Fisher information matrix for Gaussian policy π(a|s) = N(θᵀs, σ²).
"""
function gaussian_policy_fisher_information(θ, states, σ²)
    n_states, n_features = size(states)
    G = zeros(n_features, n_features)
    
    for i in 1:n_states
        s = states[i, :]
        G += s * s' / σ²  # Fisher information for Gaussian policy
    end
    
    return G / n_states
end

"""
    policy_gradient_step(θ, states, actions, rewards, baseline, σ², opt)

Perform natural policy gradient update.
"""
function policy_gradient_step(θ, states, actions, rewards, baseline, σ², opt)
    n_batch = length(rewards)
    
    # Compute advantages
    advantages = rewards .- baseline
    
    # Policy gradient
    grad = zeros(length(θ))
    for i in 1:n_batch
        s = states[i, :]
        a = actions[i]
        μ = dot(θ, s)  # Policy mean
        
        # Gradient of log π(a|s; θ)
        grad += (a - μ) * s * advantages[i] / σ²
    end
    grad /= n_batch
    
    # Fisher information matrix
    G = gaussian_policy_fisher_information(θ, states, σ²)
    G += opt.λ * I  # Regularization
    
    # Natural gradient update
    nat_grad = G \ grad
    θ_new = θ + opt.η * nat_grad
    
    return θ_new
end

"""
    train_natural_policy_gradient(env, policy_params, episodes=1000)

Train policy using natural policy gradient algorithm.
"""
function train_natural_policy_gradient(n_features, episodes=1000)
    # Initialize policy parameters
    θ = randn(n_features) * 0.1
    σ² = 1.0
    
    # Optimizer settings
    opt = NaturalPolicyGradient(0.01, 1e-3, 64)
    
    # Training statistics
    episode_rewards = Float64[]
    
    for episode in 1:episodes
        # Collect rollout data
        states = randn(opt.batch_size, n_features)
        actions = Float64[]
        rewards = Float64[]
        
        for i in 1:opt.batch_size
            s = states[i, :]
            μ = dot(θ, s)
            a = rand(Normal(μ, sqrt(σ²)))  # Sample action from policy
            r = compute_reward(s, a)  # Environment reward (placeholder)
            
            push!(actions, a)
            push!(rewards, r)
        end
        
        # Compute baseline (simple moving average)
        baseline = mean(rewards)
        
        # Natural gradient update
        θ = policy_gradient_step(θ, states, actions, rewards, baseline, σ², opt)
        
        # Record performance
        avg_reward = mean(rewards)
        push!(episode_rewards, avg_reward)
        
        if episode % 100 == 0
            println("Episode $episode: Average reward = $avg_reward")
        end
    end
    
    return θ, episode_rewards
end

# Placeholder reward function (replace with actual environment)
compute_reward(s, a) = -(s'*s + a^2) + randn() * 0.1  # Quadratic cost with noise

4.4.3 Natural Gradient Variational Inference

In variational inference, we approximate an intractable posterior p(θ|x) with a tractable variational distribution q(θ; λ). The variational parameters λ are optimized to minimize the KL divergence:

KL(q||p) = E_q[log q(θ; λ) - log p(θ|x)]

Natural gradient variational inference uses the Fisher information metric of the variational family to perform more efficient optimization.

Theorem 5.4 (Natural Gradient VI). For mean-field variational inference with factorized Gaussian approximation q(θ; λ) = ∏ᵢ N(θᵢ; μᵢ, σᵢ²), the natural gradient has a simple closed form that decouples across coordinates.

using Distributions, ForwardDiff

"""
    MeanFieldGaussianVI

Natural gradient variational inference with factorized Gaussian approximation.
"""
mutable struct MeanFieldGaussianVI
    μ::Vector{Float64}      # Variational means
    σ²::Vector{Float64}     # Variational variances
    η::Float64              # Learning rate
    n_samples::Int          # Monte Carlo samples
end

"""
    natural_gradient_vi_step!(vi, log_joint_fn)

Perform one natural gradient step for mean-field Gaussian VI.
"""
function natural_gradient_vi_step!(vi::MeanFieldGaussianVI, log_joint_fn)
    d = length(vi.μ)
    
    # Sample from current variational distribution
    samples = zeros(vi.n_samples, d)
    for i in 1:vi.n_samples
        for j in 1:d
            samples[i, j] = rand(Normal(vi.μ[j], sqrt(vi.σ²[j])))
        end
    end
    
    # Compute natural gradients
    nat_grad_μ = zeros(d)
    nat_grad_σ² = zeros(d)
    
    for i in 1:vi.n_samples
        θ = samples[i, :]
        
        # Evaluate log joint density
        log_p = log_joint_fn(θ)
        
        # Natural gradient for means (Fisher information is 1/σ² for Gaussian)
        for j in 1:d
            nat_grad_μ[j] += (log_p * (θ[j] - vi.μ[j])) / vi.σ²[j]
        end
        
        # Natural gradient for variances
        for j in 1:d
            nat_grad_σ²[j] += log_p * (0.5 * ((θ[j] - vi.μ[j])^2 / vi.σ²[j]^2 - 1 / vi.σ²[j]))
        end
    end
    
    # Average over samples and update
    nat_grad_μ ./= vi.n_samples
    nat_grad_σ² ./= vi.n_samples
    
    vi.μ += vi.η * nat_grad_μ
    vi.σ² += vi.η * nat_grad_σ²
    
    # Ensure positive variances
    vi.σ² = max.(vi.σ², 1e-6)
    
    return vi
end

"""
    run_natural_gradient_vi(log_joint_fn, d, max_iter=1000)

Run natural gradient variational inference.
"""
function run_natural_gradient_vi(log_joint_fn, d, max_iter=1000)
    # Initialize variational parameters
    vi = MeanFieldGaussianVI(
        randn(d),           # Random initial means
        ones(d),            # Unit initial variances  
        0.01,               # Learning rate
        100                 # Monte Carlo samples
    )
    
    # Track ELBO (evidence lower bound)
    elbo_history = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # Natural gradient step
        natural_gradient_vi_step!(vi, log_joint_fn)
        
        # Compute ELBO


\newpage

# Chapter 6: Information Geometry of Machine Learning

The intersection of information geometry and machine learning reveals profound connections between statistical inference and optimization on manifolds. Modern machine learning models, particularly neural networks, define statistical models whose parameter spaces form manifolds equipped with natural geometric structures. This chapter explores how information-geometric principles provide principled approaches to optimization, regularization, and architectural design in machine learning.

The key insight is that the parameter space of any parameterized statistical model forms a statistical manifold, and the Fisher information matrix provides the natural Riemannian metric. For neural networks, this geometric perspective leads to natural gradient methods that adapt step sizes according to the local curvature of the likelihood surface. For variational inference, information geometry provides the theoretical foundation for natural gradient variational Bayes. For generative adversarial networks, the framework of f-divergences connects game-theoretic training to information-geometric optimization.

## Neural Networks as Statistical Models

Every feedforward neural network defines a parameterized family of probability distributions, making it a statistical model in the information-geometric sense. Consider a neural network with parameters θ ∈ Θ ⊆ R^d that defines a conditional distribution p(y|x; θ) for outputs y given inputs x.

> **Definition 6.1 (Neural Network Statistical Manifold).** Let f_θ: R^n → R^k be a neural network with parameters θ. The neural network statistical manifold is the manifold M = {p(y|x; θ) : θ ∈ Θ} equipped with the Fisher information metric.

For classification with softmax output, the network defines:

p(y = j|x; θ) = exp(f_θ^{(j)}(x)) / Σ_{i=1}^k exp(f_θ^{(i)}(x))

where f_θ^{(j)}(x) is the j-th output of the network.

For regression with Gaussian outputs:

p(y|x; θ) = N(f_θ(x), σ²)


The Fisher information matrix for the neural network is:

G_{ij}(θ) = E_{x,y}[∂/∂θ_i log p(y|x; θ) ∂/∂θ_j log p(y|x; θ)]


### Layer-wise Block Structure of Fisher Information

The Fisher information matrix for neural networks exhibits a characteristic block structure that reflects the network architecture. Consider a feedforward network with L layers, where layer ℓ has weight matrix W^{(ℓ)} and bias vector b^{(ℓ)}.

The Fisher information can be decomposed as:

G = [G_WW G_Wb] [G_bW G_bb]

where G_WW contains weight-weight interactions, G_bb contains bias-bias interactions, and G_Wb = G_bW^T contains weight-bias cross-terms.

For deep networks, the Fisher information exhibits additional block structure corresponding to layers:

G ≈ block_diag(G^{(1)}, G^{(2)}, …, G^{(L)})

where G^{(ℓ)} is the Fisher information block for layer ℓ. This approximation becomes exact when layers are conditionally independent given their inputs, which holds approximately for many architectures.

> **Theorem 6.1 (Fisher Information Block Structure).** For a feedforward neural network with conditionally independent layers, the Fisher information matrix is block-diagonal with blocks corresponding to individual layers.

*Proof.* Let h^{(ℓ)} denote the output of layer ℓ. If layers are conditionally independent given their inputs, then:

∂ log p(y|x; θ)/∂θ^{(i)} ⊥ ∂ log p(y|x; θ)/∂θ^{(j)}

for parameters θ^{(i)}, θ^{(j)} in different layers i ≠ j, conditioned on the layer inputs. This orthogonality implies that cross-layer blocks of the Fisher information vanish. □

## K-FAC: Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature

Computing the full Fisher information matrix for neural networks is computationally prohibitive due to its O(d²) storage and computation requirements, where d is the number of parameters. The Kronecker-factored approximate curvature (K-FAC) algorithm provides a tractable approximation by exploiting the Kronecker product structure of neural network Fisher information.

### Kronecker Product Approximation

For a single layer with weight matrix W ∈ R^{m×n}, the Fisher information can be approximated as:

G_W ≈ A ⊗ S

where A ∈ R^{m×m} captures output correlations and S ∈ R^{n×n} captures input correlations, and ⊗ denotes the Kronecker product.

> **Definition 6.2 (K-FAC Approximation).** The K-FAC approximation to the Fisher information for a neural network layer with weight matrix W is G_W ≈ A ⊗ S where:
> - A = E[a a^T] with a being the pre-activation derivatives
> - S = E[s s^T] with s being the layer inputs

This factorization reduces storage from O(mn)² to O(m² + n²) and enables efficient natural gradient computation.

### Natural Gradient with K-FAC

The natural gradient descent update with K-FAC approximation is:

θ_{t+1} = θ_t - α G^{-1} ∇_θ L(θ_t)

where G^{-1} is computed using the Kronecker factorization:

(A ⊗ S)^{-1} = A^{-1} ⊗ S^{-1}


This allows efficient computation of the natural gradient without explicitly forming the full Fisher information matrix.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, Statistics

struct KFACLayer
    A::Matrix{Float64}  # Output correlation matrix
    S::Matrix{Float64}  # Input correlation matrix
    damping::Float64
end

function kfac_update!(layer::KFACLayer, inputs::Matrix, gradients::Matrix)
    # Update input correlation matrix S
    layer.S .= (1 - 0.95) * layer.S + 0.95 * (inputs * inputs')
    
    # Update output correlation matrix A  
    layer.A .= (1 - 0.95) * layer.A + 0.95 * (gradients * gradients')
    
    # Add damping for numerical stability
    layer.A .+= layer.damping * I
    layer.S .+= layer.damping * I
end

function natural_gradient(layer::KFACLayer, weight_gradient::Matrix)
    # Compute natural gradient using Kronecker factorization
    # (A ⊗ S)^{-1} vec(G) = vec(S^{-1} G A^{-1})
    A_inv = inv(layer.A)
    S_inv = inv(layer.S) 
    return S_inv * weight_gradient * A_inv
end

# Example: K-FAC for a simple neural network layer
function demo_kfac()
    # Network dimensions
    input_dim, output_dim = 100, 50
    batch_size = 32
    
    # Initialize K-FAC layer
    kfac = KFACLayer(
        Matrix{Float64}(I, output_dim, output_dim),
        Matrix{Float64}(I, input_dim, input_dim),
        1e-5
    )
    
    # Simulate training step
    inputs = randn(input_dim, batch_size)
    gradients = randn(output_dim, batch_size) 
    weight_gradient = randn(input_dim, output_dim)
    
    # Update K-FAC statistics
    kfac_update!(kfac, inputs, gradients)
    
    # Compute natural gradient
    nat_grad = natural_gradient(kfac, weight_gradient)
    
    println("Weight gradient norm: $(norm(weight_gradient))")
    println("Natural gradient norm: $(norm(nat_grad))")
    
    return nat_grad
end

demo_kfac()

4.5 Information Geometry of Variational Inference

Variational inference approximates intractable posterior distributions p(z|x) with tractable variational distributions q(z; λ) parameterized by variational parameters λ. The variational objective is to minimize the KL divergence:

L(λ) = KL(q(z; λ) || p(z|x)) = E_q[log q(z; λ)] - E_q[log p(z, x)]

From an information-geometric perspective, variational inference performs optimization on the manifold of variational distributions equipped with the Fisher information metric.

4.5.1 Natural Gradient Variational Bayes

The natural gradient of the variational objective with respect to the variational parameters is:

∇̃_λ L(λ) = G^{-1}(λ) ∇_λ L(λ)

where G(λ) is the Fisher information matrix of the variational family q(z; λ).

Theorem 6.2 (Natural Gradient for Variational Inference). The natural gradient variational Bayes update is:

λ_{t+1} = λ_t - α G^{-1}(λ_t) ∇_λ L(λ_t)

where the Fisher information matrix is G_{ij}(λ) = E_q[∂ log q(z; λ)/∂λ_i ∂ log q(z; λ)/∂λ_j].

For mean-field variational families where q(z; λ) = ∏_i q_i(z_i; λ_i), the Fisher information is block-diagonal, making natural gradient computation tractable.

4.5.2 Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows

Normalizing flows construct complex variational distributions by composing invertible transformations. Let f_θ: R^d → R^d be an invertible neural network. The flow-based variational distribution is:

q(z; θ) = p_0(f_θ^{-1}(z)) |det ∂f_θ^{-1}/∂z|

where p_0 is a simple base distribution.

The Fisher information for normalizing flows incorporates both the transformation parameters and the Jacobian determinant:

G_{ij}(θ) = E_q[∂ log q(z; θ)/∂θ_i ∂ log q(z; θ)/∂θ_j]
using Distributions, ForwardDiff

# Natural gradient for mean-field variational inference
struct MeanFieldVI
    dims::Vector{Int}
    μ::Vector{Float64}    # Mean parameters
    σ::Vector{Float64}    # Standard deviation parameters
end

function elbo_gradient(vi::MeanFieldVI, log_joint_fn, n_samples::Int=100)
    # Sample from variational distribution
    z_samples = [vi.μ + vi.σ .* randn(length(vi.μ)) for _ in 1:n_samples]
    
    # Compute ELBO gradient
    μ_grad = zeros(length(vi.μ))
    σ_grad = zeros(length(vi.σ))
    
    for z in z_samples
        # Log joint probability
        log_joint = log_joint_fn(z)
        
        # Entropy gradient
        μ_grad += (z - vi.μ) ./ (vi.σ.^2)
        σ_grad += (((z - vi.μ).^2) ./ (vi.σ.^3) - 1 ./ vi.σ)
        
        # Expected log joint gradient
        μ_grad += ForwardDiff.gradient(μ -> log_joint_fn(μ + vi.σ .* ((z - vi.μ) ./ vi.σ)), vi.μ)
    end
    
    return μ_grad / n_samples, σ_grad / n_samples
end

function fisher_information(vi::MeanFieldVI)
    # For mean-field Gaussian, Fisher information is diagonal
    G_μ = 1 ./ (vi.σ.^2)
    G_σ = 2 ./ (vi.σ.^2)
    return G_μ, G_σ
end

function natural_gradient_step!(vi::MeanFieldVI, log_joint_fn, α::Float64)
    # Compute gradients
    μ_grad, σ_grad = elbo_gradient(vi, log_joint_fn)
    
    # Compute Fisher information (diagonal for mean-field)
    G_μ, G_σ = fisher_information(vi)
    
    # Natural gradient update
    vi.μ += α * μ_grad ./ G_μ
    vi.σ += α * σ_grad ./ G_σ
    
    # Ensure positive standard deviations
    vi.σ = max.(vi.σ, 1e-6)
end

# Example: Variational inference for a bivariate Gaussian
function demo_vi()
    # True posterior is N([1, -1], [[2, 0.5], [0.5, 1]])
    true_μ = [1.0, -1.0]
    true_Σ = [2.0 0.5; 0.5 1.0]
    
    log_joint_fn = z -> logpdf(MvNormal(true_μ, true_Σ), z)
    
    # Initialize variational distribution
    vi = MeanFieldVI([1, 1], [0.0, 0.0], [1.0, 1.0])
    
    # Run natural gradient VI
    for iter in 1:1000
        natural_gradient_step!(vi, log_joint_fn, 0.01)
        
        if iter % 100 == 0
            println("Iteration $iter: μ = $(vi.μ), σ = $(vi.σ)")
        end
    end
    
    println("True mean: $true_μ")
    println("Estimated mean: $(vi.μ)")
    println("True diagonal variance: $(diag(true_Σ))")  
    println("Estimated variance: $(vi.σ.^2)")
    
    return vi
end

demo_vi()

4.6 Wasserstein Natural Gradient

The Wasserstein distance provides an alternative geometric structure on probability distributions that is particularly useful for generative modeling. The Wasserstein-2 distance between probability measures μ and ν is:

W_2(μ, ν) = (inf_{γ ∈ Π(μ,ν)} ∫ |x - y|² dγ(x,y))^{1/2}

where Π(μ, ν) is the set of joint distributions with marginals μ and ν.

For parametric families q(·; θ), the Wasserstein natural gradient uses the Wasserstein metric instead of the Fisher information metric:

∇̃_θ^W F(θ) = G_W^{-1}(θ) ∇_θ F(θ)

where G_W(θ) is the Wasserstein information matrix.

Definition 6.3 (Wasserstein Information Matrix). For a parametric family q(·; θ), the Wasserstein information matrix is:

G_W(θ) = ∫ ∇_θ T_θ(x) ∇_θ T_θ(x)^T q(x; θ) dx

where T_θ is the optimal transport map from q(·; θ) to the target distribution.

The Wasserstein natural gradient has several advantages for generative modeling: 1. It provides more stable training dynamics than standard gradients 2. It respects the geometric structure of probability distributions 3. It naturally handles mode collapse in generative models

4.6.1 Application to Generative Adversarial Networks

In the context of GANs, the Wasserstein natural gradient can be applied to the generator optimization. The generator G_θ: R^d → R^n maps noise z ~ p_z to data x = G_θ(z), inducing a distribution q_θ on the data space.

The Wasserstein GAN objective becomes:

min_θ max_φ E_{x~p_data}[D_φ(x)] - E_{z~p_z}[D_φ(G_θ(z))]

The Wasserstein natural gradient for the generator parameters incorporates the optimal transport structure:

∇̃_θ^W L = G_W^{-1}(θ) ∇_θ E_{z~p_z}[D_φ(G_θ(z))]

4.7 Information Geometry of GANs

Generative Adversarial Networks can be understood through the lens of f-divergences, connecting them to information geometry. The f-GAN framework generalizes GANs to optimize arbitrary f-divergences between the generator distribution q_θ and the data distribution p_data.

4.7.1 f-Divergence Formulation

An f-divergence between distributions P and Q is defined as:

D_f(P || Q) = ∫ q(x) f(p(x)/q(x)) dx

where f is a convex function with f(1) = 0.

Theorem 6.3 (Variational Representation of f-Divergences). Every f-divergence admits the variational representation:

D_f(P || Q) = sup_{T} [E_P[T(x)] - E_Q[f*(T(x))]]

where f* is the convex conjugate of f.

This theorem shows that optimizing f-divergences is equivalent to solving a minimax problem, which is exactly the GAN formulation.

4.7.2 Common f-Divergences in GANs

Different choices of f lead to different GAN variants:

  1. Standard GAN (Jensen-Shannon divergence): f(t) = t log t - (t+1) log(t+1)/2
  2. WGAN: Uses Wasserstein distance (not strictly an f-divergence)
  3. LSGAN: f(t) = (t-1)²/2
  4. f-GAN: General framework allowing arbitrary f

The discriminator in f-GAN approximates the optimal critic:

T*(x) = f'(p_data(x)/q_θ(x))

4.7.3 Information-Geometric Analysis of GAN Training

From an information-geometric perspective, GAN training can be viewed as gradient descent-ascent on the manifold of probability distributions. The generator performs descent on the f-divergence:

θ_{t+1} = θ_t - α ∇_θ D_f(p_data || q_θ)

The natural gradient version uses the Fisher information metric:

θ_{t+1} = θ_t - α G^{-1}(θ_t) ∇_θ D_f(p_data || q_θ)

This provides more stable training by adapting the step size according to the local curvature of the divergence.

using Flux, Distributions

# Information-geometric GAN with natural gradients
struct InfoGAN
    generator::Chain
    discriminator::Chain  
    gen_params::Vector{Float64}
    disc_params::Vector{Float64}
    fisher_reg::Float64
end

function f_divergence_loss(f_type::Symbol, real_scores, fake_scores)
    if f_type == :gan
        # Jensen-Shannon divergence (standard GAN)
        real_loss = -mean(log.(sigmoid.(real_scores)))
        fake_loss = -mean(log.(1 .- sigmoid.(fake_scores)))
        return real_loss + fake_loss
        
    elseif f_type == :wgan
        # Wasserstein distance approximation
        return mean(real_scores) - mean(fake_scores)
        
    elseif f_type == :lsgan  
        # Least squares GAN
        real_loss = mean((real_scores .- 1).^2)
        fake_loss = mean(fake_scores.^2)
        return 0.5 * (real_loss + fake_loss)
    end
end

function compute_fisher_information(model, inputs, outputs)
    # Approximate Fisher information using samples
    n_samples, n_params = length(inputs), length(Flux.params(model))
    
    # Compute score function ∇ log p(y|x; θ)
    scores = []
    for (x, y) in zip(inputs, outputs)
        grad = gradient(() -> logpdf(model(x), y), Flux.params(model))
        push!(scores, vcat([vec(g) for g in grad]...))
    end
    
    # Fisher information matrix
    score_matrix = hcat(scores...)
    return score_matrix * score_matrix' / n_samples
end

function natural_gradient_update!(params, gradients, fisher_info, α)
    # Add regularization for numerical stability
    regularized_fisher = fisher_info + 1e-6 * I
    
    # Compute natural gradient
    nat_grad = regularized_fisher \ gradients
    
    # Update parameters
    params .-= α * nat_grad
end

# Example: Training dynamics comparison
function compare_gradient_methods()
    # Simple 1D example
    true_mean = 2.0
    n_samples = 1000
    
    # Generate synthetic data
    real_data = randn(n_samples) .+ true_mean
    
    # Initialize generator (just learns a mean parameter)
    gen_mean = [0.0]  # Parameter to optimize
    
    function generator_loss(μ)
        # KL divergence from N(μ, 1) to N(2, 1)  
        return 0.5 * (μ[1] - true_mean)^2
    end
    
    # Standard gradient descent
    μ_standard = copy(gen_mean)
    standard_losses = Float64[]
    
    # Natural gradient descent  
    μ_natural = copy(gen_mean)
    natural_losses = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:100
        # Standard gradient
        grad = ForwardDiff.gradient(generator_loss, μ_standard)
        μ_standard .- α * grad
        push!(standard_losses, generator_loss(μ_standard))
        
        # Natural gradient (Fisher info = 1 for Gaussian mean)
        fisher = [1.0;;]  # 1x1 matrix
        nat_grad = fisher \ grad
        μ_natural .- α * nat_grad  
        push!(natural_losses, generator_loss(μ_natural))
    end
    
    println("Standard gradient final mean: $(μ_standard[1])")
    println("Natural gradient final mean: $(μ_natural[1])")
    println("True mean: $true_mean")
    
    return standard_losses, natural_losses
end

α = 0.1
standard_losses, natural_losses = compare_gradient_methods()

4.8 Neural Architecture and Information Geometry

Information geometry provides principled approaches to neural architecture design by analyzing the geometric properties of different architectures. The key insight is that architectures with better geometric properties—such as well-conditioned Fisher information matrices—tend to train more efficiently and generalize better.

4.8.1 Geometric Properties of Common Architectures

Different neural network architectures induce different geometric structures on the parameter manifold:

  1. Fully Connected Networks: The Fisher information exhibits block structure corresponding to layers, but can become ill-conditioned for deep networks.

  2. Convolutional Networks: Weight sharing reduces the effective dimensionality and improves conditioning of the Fisher information matrix.

  3. Residual Networks: Skip connections help preserve gradient information and maintain well-conditioned curvature throughout training.

  4. Attention Mechanisms: The attention weights create adaptive geometric structures that change based on input patterns.

4.8.2 Information-Geometric Architecture Search

Neural Architecture Search (NAS) can incorporate information-geometric criteria by selecting architectures that optimize geometric properties:

Definition 6.4 (Geometric Architecture Score). For a neural network architecture A with Fisher information matrix G_A, define the geometric score:

S_geom(A) = log det(G_A) - λ tr(G_A^{-1})

where the first term measures information content and the second term penalizes ill-conditioning.

This score balances expressivity (high determinant) with trainability (low condition number).

4.8.3 Batch Normalization and Information Geometry

Batch normalization has profound effects on the information geometry of neural networks. By normalizing layer inputs, it changes the local curvature properties:

using Statistics, LinearAlgebra

struct GeometricAnalyzer
    layer_dims::Vector{Int}
    fisher_blocks::Vector{Matrix{Float64}}
    condition_numbers::Vector{Float64}
end

function analyze_architecture_geometry(architecture_type::Symbol, depth::Int, width::Int)
    if architecture_type == :feedforward
        return analyze_feedforward_geometry(depth, width)
    elseif architecture_type == :residual  
        return analyze_residual_geometry(depth, width)
    elseif architecture_type == :attention
        return analyze_attention_geometry(depth, width)
    end
end

function analyze_feedforward_geometry(depth::Int, width::Int)
    condition_numbers = Float64[]
    
    # Simulate Fisher information for each layer
    for layer in 1:depth
        # Approximate Fisher information as random positive definite matrix
        # (In practice, this would be computed from actual network activations)
        A = randn(width, width) 
        fisher_approx = A' * A + 0.1 * I  # Add regularization
        
        cond_num = cond(fisher_approx)
        push!(condition_numbers, cond_num)
    end
    
    return GeometricAnalyzer([width for _ in 1:depth], [], condition_numbers)
end

function analyze_residual_geometry(depth::Int, width::Int)
    condition_numbers = Float64[]
    
    # Residual connections improve conditioning
    for layer in 1:depth
        A = randn(width, width)
        # Residual connections act like regularization
        fisher_approx = A' * A + (1.0 + 0.1*layer) * I  
        
        cond_num = cond(fisher_approx)
        push!(condition_numbers, cond_num)
    end
    
    return GeometricAnalyzer([width for _ in 1:depth], [], condition_numbers)
end

function geometric_architecture_score(analyzer::GeometricAnalyzer, λ::Float64=1.0)
    # Combine conditioning and expressivity measures
    avg_condition = mean(analyzer.condition_numbers)
    stability_penalty = λ * log(avg_condition)
    
    # Higher dimensional layers contribute more to expressivity
    expressivity_bonus = sum(log.(analyzer.layer_dims))
    
    return expressivity_bonus - stability_penalty
end

function compare_architectures()
    println("Comparing information-geometric properties of neural architectures:")
    
    depth, width = 10, 100
    
    # Analyze different architectures
    feedforward = analyze_feedforward_geometry(depth, width)
    residual = analyze_residual_geometry(depth, width)
    
    # Compute geometric scores
    ff_score = geometric_architecture_score(feedforward)
    res_score = geometric_architecture_score(residual)
    
    println("\nFeedforward Network:")
    println("  Average condition number: $(mean(feedforward.condition_numbers))")
    println("  Geometric score: $ff_score")
    
    println("\nResidual Network:")  
    println("  Average condition number: $(mean(residual.condition_numbers))")
    println("  Geometric score: $res_score")
    
    println("\nResidual networks show better geometric properties!")
    
    return feedforward, residual
end

compare_architectures()

4.8.4 Information Bottleneck and Architecture Design

The information bottleneck principle provides another information-theoretic approach to architecture design. A neural network layer should preserve task-relevant information while discarding irrelevant details.

For a layer with input X and output Y, the information bottleneck objective is:

min I(X; Y) - β I(Y; T)

where T is the target variable and β controls the trade-off between compression and prediction.

This principle suggests that optimal architectures should have: 1. Sufficient capacity to capture task-relevant information 2. Appropriate bottlenecks to prevent overfitting 3. Smooth information flow throughout the network

4.9 Connections to Optimization and Generalization

Information geometry provides fundamental insights into why certain optimization algorithms and architectural choices lead to better generalization. The key connections are:

  1. Generalization via Geometry: Flatter minima in the loss landscape correspond to better generalization, and information geometry quantifies this flatness through the Fisher information.

  2. Natural Gradients and Generalization: Natural gradient methods implicitly regularize toward flatter regions of parameter space, improving generalization.

  3. Architecture and Inductive Bias: Different architectures encode different geometric structures, which serve as inductive biases for learning.

The geometric perspective unifies these phenomena under a common mathematical framework, providing principled approaches to algorithm and architecture design in machine learning.

4.10 Exercises

  1. Fisher Information Computation: For a two-layer neural network with ReLU activations and softmax output, derive the explicit form of the Fisher information matrix. Show that it has block structure corresponding to the layers.

  2. K-FAC Implementation: Implement the K-FAC algorithm for a convolutional neural network layer. Compare convergence rates with standard gradient descent on CIFAR-10 classification.

  3. Variational Inference: Implement natural gradient variational Bayes for a mixture of Gaussians model. Compare with standard coordinate ascent variational inference.

  4. GAN f-Divergences: Implement f-GAN with three different f-divergences: Jensen-Shannon, KL, and squared Hellinger. Compare generated sample quality and training stability.

  5. Wasserstein Natural Gradient: Derive the Wasserstein information matrix for a simple parametric family (e.g., location-scale Gaussians). Implement Wasserstein natural gradient descent.

  6. Architecture Analysis: Design an information-geometric score for comparing neural architectures that incorporates both Fisher information conditioning and expressivity. Apply to compare different ResNet variants.

  7. Batch Normalization Geometry: Analyze how batch normalization affects the Fisher information matrix of a deep network. Provide both theoretical analysis and empirical verification.

  8. Natural Gradient Flow: Prove that natural gradient descent follows the steepest descent direction in the Fisher information metric. Show convergence guarantees under appropriate conditions.

  9. Information Bottleneck: Implement the information bottleneck principle for neural network layer design. Show how it leads to architectures with better generalization properties.

  10. Geometric Regularization: Design a regularization term based on the condition number of the Fisher information matrix. Show that minimizing this term improves training stability for deep networks.

4.11 References

Amari, S. (1998). Natural gradient works efficiently in learning. Neural Computation, 10(2), 251-276.

Martens, J., & Grosse, R. (2015). Optimizing neural networks with Kronecker-factored approximate curvature. International Conference on Machine Learning, 2408-2417.

Nowozin, S., Cseke, B., & Tomioka, R. (2016). f-GAN: Training generative neural samplers using variational divergence minimization. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 271-279.

Hoffman, M. D., Blei, D. M., Wang, C., & Paisley, J. (2013). Stochastic variational inference. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 14(1), 1303-1347.

Li, C., Chen, C., Carlson, D., & Carin, L. (2016). Preconditioned stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics for deep neural networks. AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence.

Arjovsky, M., Chintala, S., & Bottou, L. (2017). Wasserstein generative adversarial networks. International Conference on Machine Learning, 214-223.

Zhang, G., Sun, S., Duvenaud, D., & Grosse, R. (2018). Noisy natural gradients as variational inference. International Conference on Machine Learning, 5852-5861.

Tishby, N., &

5 Chapter 7: Information Geometry and Thermodynamics

The profound connection between information geometry and thermodynamics reveals one of the most elegant unifications in mathematical physics. Statistical mechanics naturally produces exponential families of probability distributions, while the Fisher information metric on these manifolds encodes thermodynamic properties like heat capacity and susceptibility. Phase transitions correspond to singularities of the metric, and the scalar curvature captures critical phenomena. This geometric perspective provides new insights into optimization of thermodynamic processes and the fundamental limits of information processing in physical systems.

5.1 7.1 Gibbs Distributions as Exponential Families

Thermodynamic systems in equilibrium are described by Gibbs distributions, which form exponential families parameterized by inverse temperature and chemical potentials. This connection places statistical mechanics squarely within the framework of information geometry.

Definition 7.1 (Gibbs Distribution). Let H(x;θ) be a Hamiltonian function on state space Ω with parameters θ. The Gibbs distribution at inverse temperature β is

p(x;β,θ) = exp(-βH(x;θ) - ψ(β,θ))

where ψ(β,θ) = log Z(β,θ) is the log partition function and Z(β,θ) = ∫_Ω exp(-βH(x;θ))dx.

The Gibbs distribution is an exponential family with sufficient statistics T(x) = -H(x;θ) and natural parameter η = β. The cumulant generating function is precisely the log partition function ψ(β,θ).

For a system with Hamiltonian H(x) = ∑ᵢ Eᵢnᵢ(x), where Eᵢ are energy levels and nᵢ(x) are occupation numbers, the Gibbs distribution becomes

p(x;β,μ) = exp(-β∑ᵢ Eᵢnᵢ(x) + β∑ᵢ μᵢnᵢ(x) - ψ(β,μ))

where μᵢ are chemical potentials. This is an exponential family with sufficient statistics T(x) = (∑ᵢ Eᵢnᵢ(x), n₁(x), …, nₖ(x)) and natural parameters η = (-β, βμ₁, …, βμₖ).

Theorem 7.1 (Thermodynamic Potentials as Cumulants). For a Gibbs distribution, the cumulants of the log partition function yield thermodynamic potentials: - Internal energy: U = -∂ψ/∂β - Entropy: S = βU + ψ
- Heat capacity: C = β²∂²ψ/∂β² - Particle number: Nᵢ = ∂ψ/∂μᵢ

Proof. These follow directly from the moment generating property of exponential families. The internal energy is U = E[H] = -∂ψ/∂β. The entropy is S = -E[log p] = βU + ψ. Heat capacity emerges as C = ∂U/∂T = -β²∂²ψ/∂β² where T = 1/β is temperature. Particle numbers are Nᵢ = E[nᵢ] = ∂ψ/∂μᵢ. □

5.1.1 Computational Example: Ideal Gas

Consider an ideal gas with energy levels E(n₁,n₂,n₃) = (n₁² + n₂² + n₃²)/(2m) for quantum numbers nᵢ. The partition function and thermodynamic properties can be computed using the exponential family structure.

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff

"""
Ideal gas partition function and thermodynamic quantities
"""
struct IdealGas
    mass::Float64
    volume::Float64
    max_n::Int  # Maximum quantum number for numerical computation
end

function log_partition_function(gas::IdealGas, β::Float64)
    # Approximate partition function by summing over finite quantum states
    Z = 0.0
    for n1 in 1:gas.max_n, n2 in 1:gas.max_n, n3 in 1:gas.max_n
        energy = (n1^2 + n2^2 + n3^2) / (2 * gas.mass)
        Z += exp(-β * energy)
    end
    return log(Z) + log(gas.volume)  # Volume factor
end

function thermodynamic_properties(gas::IdealGas, β::Float64)
    ψ = log_partition_function(gas, β)
    
    # Use automatic differentiation for derivatives
    U = -ForwardDiff.derivative(β -> log_partition_function(gas, β), β)
    C = β^2 * ForwardDiff.derivative(β -> -ForwardDiff.derivative(
        β -> log_partition_function(gas, β), β), β)
    
    T = 1/β
    S = β * U + ψ
    
    return (temperature=T, internal_energy=U, heat_capacity=C, entropy=S)
end

# Example calculation
gas = IdealGas(1.0, 1.0, 20)  # Mass=1, Volume=1, max_n=20
β_range = range(0.1, 5.0, length=50)

properties = [thermodynamic_properties(gas, β) for β in β_range]
temperatures = [p.temperature for p in properties]
heat_capacities = [p.heat_capacity for p in properties]

println("Temperature range: $(extrema(temperatures))")
println("Heat capacity range: $(extrema(heat_capacities))")

5.2 7.2 Temperature as a Coordinate on the Thermodynamic Manifold

Temperature naturally emerges as a coordinate on the statistical manifold of Gibbs distributions. The choice of parameterization—whether by temperature T or inverse temperature β—affects the geometric structure and has physical significance.

Consider a one-parameter family of Gibbs distributions p(x;β) with Hamiltonian H(x). The statistical manifold has natural coordinate β (inverse temperature) or alternative coordinate T = 1/β (temperature). The Fisher information metric takes different forms in each parameterization.

Definition 7.2 (Fisher Metric in Temperature Coordinates). For a Gibbs distribution p(x;β) with β = 1/T, the Fisher information metric in temperature coordinates is

g_TT = (1/T⁴) Var[H] = C/T²

where C is the heat capacity and Var[H] is the variance of the Hamiltonian.

The factor T⁻² relating the Fisher metric to heat capacity has deep physical meaning: it measures the geometric “distance” between thermal states and connects information geometry to thermodynamic stability.

Theorem 7.2 (Thermodynamic Stability and Fisher Metric). A thermodynamic system is stable if and only if the Fisher information metric is positive definite. Instability corresponds to metric degeneracy.

Proof. Thermodynamic stability requires C = β²∂²ψ/∂β² > 0, which is equivalent to positive definiteness of the Fisher metric g_ββ = Var[H] > 0. Metric degeneracy (g = 0) occurs when Var[H] = 0, indicating all microstates have identical energy—an unstable configuration. □

5.2.1 Example: Paramagnetic System

Consider a paramagnetic system with Hamiltonian H = -μBM where μ is magnetic moment, B is magnetic field, and M is magnetization. The Gibbs distribution is

p(M;β,B) = exp(βμBM - ψ(β,B))

The Fisher information metric in the (β,B) coordinate system is

g = [Var[H] -Cov[H,μM]] [-Cov[H,μM] μ²Var[M] ]

where Var[H] = μ²B²Var[M] is the energy variance and Var[M] is the magnetization variance (magnetic susceptibility).

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra

"""
Paramagnetic system with spin-1/2 particles
"""
function paramagnetic_system(N::Int, β::Float64, B::Float64, μ::Float64=1.0)
    # For N spin-1/2 particles, magnetization M can range from -N/2 to N/2
    M_values = collect(-N/2:1:N/2)
    
    # Energy for each magnetization state
    energies = [-μ * B * M for M in M_values]
    
    # Gibbs probabilities
    log_probs = β * μ * B * M_values
    log_Z = logsumexp(log_probs)
    probs = exp.(log_probs .- log_Z)
    
    # Thermodynamic quantities
    avg_M = sum(M_values .* probs)
    avg_H = -μ * B * avg_M
    var_M = sum(M_values.^2 .* probs) - avg_M^2
    var_H = (μ * B)^2 * var_M
    
    # Fisher metric components
    g_ββ = var_H
    g_βB = -μ * (-μ * B * var_M)  # Covariance term
    g_BB = μ^2 * var_H / (μ * B)^2  # Simplified
    
    fisher_metric = [g_ββ g_βB; g_βB g_BB]
    
    return (
        magnetization=avg_M,
        energy=avg_H,
        susceptibility=var_M,
        fisher_metric=fisher_metric,
        determinant=det(fisher_metric)
    )
end

# Study Fisher metric as function of temperature
N = 100  # Number of spins
B = 1.0  # Magnetic field
β_values = range(0.1, 5.0, length=100)

results = [paramagnetic_system(N, β, B) for β in β_values]
temperatures = 1 ./ β_values
susceptibilities = [r.susceptibility for r in results]
metric_dets = [r.determinant for r in results]

println("Peak susceptibility at T = $(temperatures[argmax(susceptibilities)])")
println("Fisher metric determinant range: $(extrema(metric_dets))")

5.3 7.3 Fisher Metric and Heat Capacity

The profound connection between the Fisher information metric and heat capacity provides a geometric interpretation of thermodynamic response functions. Heat capacity measures the system’s resistance to temperature change, while the Fisher metric measures the distinguishability of nearby thermal states.

For a system with Hamiltonian H(x) and inverse temperature β, the Fisher information is

g_ββ = ∫ p(x;β) (∂ log p(x;β)/∂β)² dx = Var_β[H] = ⟨H²⟩ - ⟨H⟩²

The heat capacity is C = ∂⟨H⟩/∂T = β²Var_β[H] = β²g_ββ, establishing the fundamental relationship

C = β²g_ββ

This equation reveals that heat capacity is the Fisher metric scaled by β². Systems with large heat capacity have thermal states that are highly distinguishable (large Fisher metric), while systems with small heat capacity have thermal states that are geometrically close.

Theorem 7.3 (Geometric Interpretation of Specific Heat). The specific heat per particle c = C/N satisfies

c = (β²/N) g_ββ = (β²/N) Var[H]

The Fisher metric per particle g_ββ/N measures the average information gained per particle about the inverse temperature β from a single observation.

Proof. For N independent particles, the total energy variance is N times the single-particle variance (assuming no correlations). The Fisher information scales as Var[H_total] = N·Var[H_single], so g_ββ/N = Var[H_single] represents the information per particle. □

5.3.1 Critical Phenomena and Metric Singularities

Near phase transitions, thermodynamic response functions diverge, corresponding to singularities in the Fisher metric. Consider the critical behavior of heat capacity near a second-order phase transition at temperature T_c:

C ~ |T - T_c|^(-α)

where α is the critical exponent. The Fisher metric exhibits the same singularity:

g_TT = C/T² ~ |T - T_c|^(-α)

This geometric singularity reflects the diverging correlations at the critical point. The Fisher metric becomes degenerate exactly at the transition, indicating that thermal states become indistinguishable at the critical temperature.

using Plots

"""
Ising model mean field approximation for critical behavior
"""
function ising_mean_field(T::Float64, J::Float64=1.0, h::Float64=0.0)
    # Mean field critical temperature
    Tc = J
    
    if T > Tc
        # Paramagnetic phase: m = 0
        m = 0.0
        χ = 1 / (T - Tc)  # Susceptibility diverges as T → Tc⁺
    else
        # Ferromagnetic phase: solve m = tanh((Jm + h)/T)
        m_guess = sqrt(1 - T/Tc)  # Initial guess near Tc
        for _ in 1:100  # Fixed point iteration
            m_new = tanh((J * m_guess + h) / T)
            if abs(m_new - m_guess) < 1e-10
                break
            end
            m_guess = 0.5 * (m_new + m_guess)  # Damped iteration
        end
        m = m_guess
        χ = (1 - m^2) / (T - J * (1 - m^2))
    end
    
    # Heat capacity (simplified mean field result)
    if abs(T - Tc) < 1e-8
        C = 1000.0  # Large value at critical point
    else
        C = (J * m)^2 / T^2 * χ
    end
    
    # Fisher metric component
    g_TT = C / T^2
    
    return (magnetization=m, susceptibility=χ, heat_capacity=C, fisher_metric=g_TT)
end

# Study critical behavior
J = 1.0
Tc = J
T_range = [range(0.1, Tc-0.01, length=50); range(Tc+0.01, 3.0, length=50)]

results = [ising_mean_field(T, J) for T in T_range]
heat_capacities = [r.heat_capacity for r in results]
fisher_metrics = [r.fisher_metric for r in results]

# Plot critical behavior
p1 = plot(T_range, heat_capacities, xlabel="Temperature", ylabel="Heat Capacity", 
          title="Critical Behavior", yscale=:log10, ylims=(0.1, 1000))
vline!([Tc], label="Tc", linestyle=:dash)

p2 = plot(T_range, fisher_metrics, xlabel="Temperature", ylabel="Fisher Metric", 
          yscale=:log10, ylims=(0.001, 100))
vline!([Tc], label="Tc", linestyle=:dash)

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(800,400))

5.4 7.4 Phase Transitions as Metric Singularities

Phase transitions manifest as singularities in the Fisher information metric, providing a geometric characterization of critical phenomena. The order of the phase transition corresponds to the type of metric singularity, while critical exponents determine the scaling behavior near the transition.

Definition 7.3 (Thermodynamic Phase Transition). A phase transition occurs at parameter values where the Fisher information metric becomes degenerate (det g = 0) or where its derivatives diverge.

For a first-order phase transition, the first derivatives of thermodynamic potentials are discontinuous, but the potentials themselves remain analytic. The Fisher metric exhibits a discontinuous jump but remains finite.

For a second-order phase transition, the second derivatives of thermodynamic potentials diverge. This corresponds to divergence of the Fisher metric components, indicating that thermal states become infinitely distinguishable near the critical point.

5.4.1 Example: Van der Waals Gas

The van der Waals equation of state provides a classic example of both first-order (liquid-gas) and second-order (critical point) transitions. The equation of state is

(P + a/V²)(V - b) = RT

where a and b are van der Waals parameters. The critical point occurs at (P_c, V_c, T_c) with

P_c = a/(27b²), V_c = 3b, T_c = 8a/(27Rb)

Near the critical point, thermodynamic response functions exhibit power-law scaling, corresponding to algebraic singularities in the Fisher metric.

Theorem 7.4 (Fisher Metric Scaling at Critical Points). Near a critical point with critical exponents α (heat capacity) and γ (susceptibility), the Fisher metric components scale as

g_TT ~ |t|^(-α), g_hh ~ |t|^(-γ)

where t = (T - T_c)/T_c is the reduced temperature and h is the conjugate field.

Proof. The scaling follows from the correspondence between Fisher metric components and thermodynamic response functions. Heat capacity C ~ |t|^(-α) gives g_TT = C/T² ~ |t|^(-α). Similarly, susceptibility χ ~ |t|^(-γ) corresponds to g_hh ~ |t|^(-γ). □

using Roots, ForwardDiff

"""
Van der Waals gas with critical behavior
"""
struct VanDerWaals
    a::Float64  # Attraction parameter
    b::Float64  # Excluded volume parameter  
    R::Float64  # Gas constant
end

function critical_constants(vdw::VanDerWaals)
    Pc = vdw.a / (27 * vdw.b^2)
    Vc = 3 * vdw.b
    Tc = 8 * vdw.a / (27 * vdw.R * vdw.b)
    return (Pc=Pc, Vc=Vc, Tc=Tc)
end

function pressure(vdw::VanDerWaals, V::Float64, T::Float64)
    return vdw.R * T / (V - vdw.b) - vdw.a / V^2
end

function compressibility(vdw::VanDerWaals, V::Float64, T::Float64)
    # Isothermal compressibility κ = -1/V * (∂V/∂P)_T
    dPdV = ForwardDiff.derivative(V -> pressure(vdw, V, T), V)
    return -1 / (V * dPdV)
end

function heat_capacity_V(vdw::VanDerWaals, V::Float64, T::Float64)
    # For ideal gas component: Cv = (3/2)R per mole
    # Van der Waals correction is typically small
    return 1.5 * vdw.R  # Simplified constant heat capacity
end

function fisher_metric_components(vdw::VanDerWaals, V::Float64, T::Float64)
    κ = compressibility(vdw, V, T)
    Cv = heat_capacity_V(vdw, V, T)
    
    # Fisher metric in (T,V) coordinates
    g_TT = Cv / T^2
    g_VV = κ / V^2  # Simplified form
    g_TV = 0.0  # Assume uncorrelated for simplicity
    
    return [g_TT g_TV; g_TV g_VV]
end

# Study critical behavior
vdw = VanDerWaals(1.0, 0.1, 1.0)  # Example parameters
crit = critical_constants(vdw)

# Temperature scan near critical point
T_range = range(crit.Tc * 0.8, crit.Tc * 1.2, length=100)
V_fixed = crit.Vc  # Fix volume near critical

results = []
for T in T_range
    try
        g = fisher_metric_components(vdw, V_fixed, T)
        κ = compressibility(vdw, V_fixed, T)
        push!(results, (T=T, fisher_metric=g, compressibility=κ, det_g=det(g)))
    catch
        # Skip problematic points
        continue
    end
end

temperatures = [r.T for r in results]
compressibilities = [abs(r.compressibility) for r in results]
metric_determinants = [abs(r.det_g) for r in results]

println("Critical temperature: $(crit.Tc)")
println("Temperature range: $(extrema(temperatures))")
println("Max compressibility: $(maximum(compressibilities))")

5.5 7.5 Scalar Curvature and Critical Phenomena

The scalar curvature of the Fisher information metric provides a geometric measure of criticality. Near phase transitions, the scalar curvature often diverges, encoding universal features of critical phenomena that are independent of microscopic details.

For a statistical manifold with Fisher metric g_ij, the scalar curvature R encodes the intrinsic curvature at each point. Near critical points, this curvature typically exhibits scaling behavior related to critical exponents.

Definition 7.4 (Thermodynamic Scalar Curvature). For a thermodynamic system with Fisher metric g, the scalar curvature R measures the deviation from flatness of the statistical manifold. Near a critical point with correlation length ξ ~ |t|^(-ν), the curvature scales as

R ~ ξ^(-2) ~ |t|^(2ν)

This scaling relationship connects the geometric curvature to the physical correlation length, providing a bridge between information geometry and renormalization group theory.

5.5.1 Curvature in Two-Parameter Systems

Consider a two-parameter thermodynamic system with parameters θ¹ = T (temperature) and θ² = h (external field). The Fisher metric is

g = [g_TT g_Th] [g_Th g_hh]

The scalar curvature is given by

R = -2g^(-1/2) ∂/∂θⁱ (g^(1/2) g^(ij) Γ^k_(ik))

where Γ^k_(ij) are the Christoffel symbols. Near a critical point, this curvature diverges with characteristic scaling.

Theorem 7.5 (Curvature Universality). Systems in the same universality class exhibit identical scaling behavior of the scalar curvature near critical points, regardless of microscopic details.

Proof. The universality follows from the renormalization group property that critical exponents depend only on dimensionality and symmetries, not on microscopic parameters. Since curvature scaling is determined by critical exponents through R ~ |t|^(2ν), systems with identical exponents have identical curvature scaling. □

using LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff

"""
Scalar curvature computation for 2D Fisher metric
"""
function scalar_curvature_2d(g::Matrix{Float64}, dg_dtheta1::Matrix{Float64}, 
                            dg_dtheta2::Matrix{Float64})
    # For 2D manifold, scalar curvature has closed form
    det_g = det(g)
    g_inv = inv(g)
    
    if det_g <= 1e-12
        return Inf  # Singular metric
    end
    
    # Christoffel symbols (simplified calculation)
    # This is a simplified implementation for demonstration
    # Full calculation would involve all second derivatives
    
    # Use Gauss curvature formula for 2D case
    # K = (1/sqrt(det_g)) * ∂²/∂θ¹∂θ² log(sqrt(det_g))
    
    # Approximate using finite differences of metric determinant
    trace_term = tr(g_inv * dg_dtheta1) * tr(g_inv * dg_dtheta2)
    cross_term = tr(g_inv * dg_dtheta1 * g_inv * dg_dtheta2)
    
    K = (trace_term - cross_term) / (2 * det_g)
    
    return K  # Gaussian curvature = scalar curvature in 2D
end

"""
Example: Curvature of Ising model near critical point
"""
function ising_curvature(T::Float64, h::Float64, J::Float64=1.0)
    Tc = J
    
    # Simplified Fisher metric for Ising model in (T,h) coordinates
    # Near critical point: χ ~ |T-Tc|^(-γ), C ~ |T-Tc|^(-α)
    
    t = (T - Tc) / Tc  # Reduced temperature
    
    if abs(t) < 1e-6
        # At critical point
        g_TT = 1000.0  # Large heat capacity
        g_hh = 1000.0  # Large susceptibility  
        g_Th = 0.0
    else
        # Critical scaling: α ≈ 0, γ ≈ 1 for mean field
        g_TT = abs(t)^(-0.1)  # Heat capacity scaling
        g_hh = abs(t)^(-1.0)  # Susceptibility scaling
        g_Th = 0.1 * g_TT^0.5 * g_hh^0.5  # Small cross term
    end
    
    g = [g_TT g_Th; g_Th g_hh]
    
    # Approximate derivatives with finite differences
    δ = 1e-6
    g_plus_T = [abs(t+δ)^(-0.1) g_Th; g_Th abs(t)^(-1.0)]
    g_plus_h = [g_TT g_Th; g_Th abs(t)^(-1.0)]  # h-derivative 
    
    dg_dT = (g_plus_T - g) / δ
    dg_dh = (g_plus_h - g) / δ
    
    try
        K = scalar_curvature_2d(g, dg_dT, dg_dh)
        return (curvature=K, metric=g, det_metric=det(g))
    catch
        return (curvature=NaN, metric=g, det_metric=det(g))
    end
end

# Study curvature near critical point
Tc = 1.0
T_range = [range(0.5, Tc-0.01, length=20); range(Tc+0.01, 1.5, length=20)]
h_fixed = 0.0  # Zero field

curvature_results = [ising_curvature(T, h_fixed) for T in T_range]
curvatures = [r.curvature for r in curvature_results if isfinite(r.curvature)]
valid_temps = [T_range[i] for (i,r) in enumerate(curvature_results) if isfinite(r.curvature)]

if !isempty(curvatures)
    println("Curvature range: $(extrema(curvatures))")
    println("Peak curvature at T ≈ $(valid_temps[argmax(abs.(curvatures))])")
end

5.6 7.6 Thermodynamic Length and Optimal Protocols

Information geometry provides a natural framework for defining thermodynamic length and optimizing finite-time processes. The Fisher metric defines a Riemannian distance on the space of thermal states, leading to geodesics that represent optimal thermodynamic protocols.

Definition 7.5 (Thermodynamic Length). For a thermodynamic process connecting states with parameters θ(0) and θ(τ), the thermodynamic length is

L[θ(·)] = ∫₀ᵗ √(g_ij(θ(t)) θ̇ⁱ(t) θ̇ʲ(t)) dt

where g_ij is the Fisher information metric and θ̇ⁱ = dθⁱ/dt.

The thermodynamic length generalizes the concept of geometric distance to parameter space. Shorter paths correspond to processes that maintain better thermodynamic efficiency by staying close to equilibrium.

5.6.1 Optimal Control and Geodesics

The optimal thermodynamic protocol between two states minimizes the thermodynamic length, yielding geodesics of the Fisher metric. These geodesics satisfy the geodesic equation

d²θᵏ/dt² + Γᵏᵢⱼ (dθⁱ/dt)(dθʲ/dt) = 0

where Γᵏᵢⱼ are Christoffel symbols of the Fisher metric.

Theorem 7.6 (Optimal Thermodynamic Protocols). The geodesics of the Fisher information metric minimize the excess work performed in finite-time thermodynamic processes, subject to the constraint of fixed process duration.

Proof. The excess work in a finite-time process is related to the deviation from quasi-static equilibrium. This deviation is measured by the thermodynamic length, which quantifies how “fast” the system is driven through parameter space. Geodesics minimize this length and therefore minimize excess work. □

5.6.2 Example: Optimal Heating Protocol

Consider heating a system from temperature T₁ to T₂ in time τ. For a simple system with Fisher metric g_TT = C/T², the optimal temperature protocol T(t) minimizes

L = ∫₀ᵗ √(C(T(t))/T(t)²) |dT/dt| dt

The Euler-Lagrange equation yields the geodesic equation for optimal heating.

using DifferentialEquations, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

"""
Optimal thermodynamic protocol computation
"""
function optimal_heating_protocol(T1::Float64, T2::Float64, τ::Float64, 
                                 heat_capacity::Function)
    """
    Find optimal temperature protocol T(t) for heating from T1 to T2 in time τ
    """
    
    # Parameterize T(t) with spline basis functions
    n_control = 10  # Number of control points
    t_points = range(0, τ, length=n_control)
    
    function temperature_protocol(p::Vector, t::Float64)
        # Linear interpolation between control points
        if t <= 0
            return T1
        elseif t >= τ
            return T2
        else
            # Find bracketing points
            idx = searchsortedfirst(t_points, t)
            if idx == 1
                return T1
            elseif idx > length(t_points)
                return T2
            else
                t_left, t_right = t_points[idx-1], t_points[idx]
                T_left = idx == 2 ? T1 : p[idx-2]
                T_right = idx == length(t_points) ? T2 : p[idx-1]
